distinction. For example you might use
<span class="calibre28">`a`</span> for all local variables and
<span class="calibre28">`the`</span> for all function
arguments.<sup>3</sup> The problem comes in when you decide to call a
variable <span class="calibre28">`theZork`</span> because you already
have another variable named <span class="calibre28">`zork`</span>.

<span class="calibre34">3. Uncle Bob used to do this in C++ but has
given up the practice because modern IDEs make it unnecessary.</span>

Noise words are redundant. The word
<span class="calibre28">`variable`</span> should never appear in a
variable name. The word <span class="calibre28">`table`</span> should
never appear in a table name. How is
<span class="calibre28">`NameString`</span> better than
<span class="calibre28">`Name`</span>? Would a
<span class="calibre28">`Name`</span> ever be a floating point number?
If so, it breaks an earlier rule about disinformation. Imagine finding
one class named <span class="calibre28">`Customer`</span> and another
named <span class="calibre28">`CustomerObject`</span>. What should you
understand as the distinction? Which one will represent the best path to
a customer’s payment history?

There is an application we know of where this is illustrated. we’ve
changed the names to protect the guilty, but here’s the exact form of
the error:

<span class="calibre28">   getActiveAccount();  
   getActiveAccounts();  
   getActiveAccountInfo();</span>

How are the programmers in this project supposed to know which of these
functions to call?

In the absence of specific conventions, the variable
<span class="calibre28">`moneyAmount`</span> is indistinguishable from
<span class="calibre28">`money, customerInfo`</span> is
indistinguishable from
<span class="calibre28">`customer, accountData`</span> is
indistinguishable from <span class="calibre28">`account`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`theMessage`</span> is indistinguishable from
<span class="calibre28">`message`</span>. Distinguish names in such a
way that the reader knows what the differences offer.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_024.html_filepos153658"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_024.html_calibre_pb_33" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_025.html"></span>

### **Use Pronounceable Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Humans are good at words. A significant part of our brains is dedicated
to the concept of words. And words are, by definition, pronounceable. It
would be a shame not to take
<span id="Clean_Code_split_025.html_filepos153999"
class="calibre2"></span>advantage of that huge portion of our brains
that has evolved to deal with spoken language. So make your names
pronounceable.

If you can’t pronounce it, you can’t discuss it without sounding like an
idiot. “Well, over here on the bee cee arr three cee enn tee we have a
pee ess zee kyew int, see?” This matters because programming is a social
activity.

A company I know has <span class="calibre28">`genymdhms`</span>
(generation date, year, month, day, hour, minute, and second) so they
walked around saying “gen why emm dee aich emm ess”. I have an annoying
habit of pronouncing everything as written, so I started saying
“gen-yah-muddahims.” It later was being called this by a host of
designers and analysts, and we still sounded silly. But we were in on
the joke, so it was fun. Fun or not, we were tolerating poor naming. New
developers had to have the variables explained to them, and then they
spoke about it in silly made-up words instead of using proper English
terms. Compare

<span class="calibre28">   class DtaRcrd102 {  
     private Date genymdhms;  
     private Date modymdhms;  
     private final String pszqint = ”102”;  
      /\* … \*/  
   };</span>

to

<span class="calibre28">   class Customer {  
     private Date generationTimestamp;  
     private Date modificationTimestamp;;  
     private final String recordId = ”102”;  
     /\* … \*/  
   };</span>

Intelligent conversation is now possible: “Hey, Mikey, take a look at
this record! The generation timestamp is set to tomorrow’s date! How can
that be?”

<span id="Clean_Code_split_025.html_filepos156300"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_025.html_calibre_pb_34" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_026.html"></span>

### **Use Searchable Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Single-letter names and numeric constants have a particular problem in
that they are not easy to locate across a body of text.

One might easily grep for
<span class="calibre28">`MAX_CLASSES_PER_STUDENT`</span>, but the number
7 could be more troublesome. Searches may turn up the digit as part of
file names, other constant definitions, and in various expressions where
the value is used with different intent. It is even worse when a
constant is a long number and someone might have transposed digits,
thereby creating a bug while simultaneously evading the programmer’s
search.

Likewise, the name <span class="calibre28">`e`</span> is a poor choice
for any variable for which a programmer might need to search. It is the
most common letter in the English language and likely to show up in
every passage of text in every program. In this regard, longer names
trump shorter names, and any searchable name trumps a constant in code.

My personal preference is that single-letter names can ONLY be used as
local variables inside short methods. *The length of a name should
correspond to the size of its scope*
<span id="Clean_Code_split_026.html_filepos157756"
class="calibre2"></span>\[N5\]. If a variable or constant might be seen
or used in multiple places in a body of code, it is imperative to give
it a search-friendly name. Once again compare

<span class="calibre28">   for (int j=0; j\<34; j++) {  
     s += (t\[j\]\*4)/5;  
   }</span>

to

<span class="calibre28">   int realDaysPerIdealDay = 4;  
   const int WORK_DAYS_PER_WEEK = 5;  
   int sum = 0;  
   for (int j=0; j \< NUMBER_OF_TASKS; j++) {  
     int realTaskDays = taskEstimate\[j\] \* realDaysPerIdealDay;  
     int realTaskWeeks = (realdays / WORK_DAYS_PER_WEEK);  
     sum += realTaskWeeks;  
   }</span>

Note that <span class="calibre28">`sum`</span>, above, is not a
particularly useful name but at least is searchable. The intentionally
named code makes for a longer function, but consider how much easier it
will be to find <span class="calibre28">`WORK_DAYS_PER_WEEK`</span> than
to find all the places where 5 was used and filter the list down to just
the instances with the intended meaning.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_026.html_filepos159238"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_026.html_calibre_pb_35" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_027.html"></span>

### **Avoid Encodings**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

We have enough encodings to deal with without adding more to our burden.
Encoding type or scope information into names simply adds an extra
burden of deciphering. It hardly seems reasonable to require each new
employee to learn yet another encoding “language” in addition to
learning the (usually considerable) body of code that they’ll be working
in. It is an unnecessary mental burden when trying to solve a problem.
Encoded names are seldom pronounceable and are easy to mis-type.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_027.html_filepos159955"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Hungarian Notation**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In days of old, when we worked in name-length-challenged languages, we
violated this rule out of necessity, and with regret. Fortran forced
encodings by making the first letter a code for the type. Early versions
of BASIC allowed only a letter plus one digit. Hungarian Notation (HN)
took this to a whole new level.

HN was considered to be pretty important back in the Windows C API, when
everything was an integer handle or a long pointer or a
<span class="calibre28">`void`</span> pointer, or one of several
implementations of “string” (with different uses and attributes). The
compiler did not check types in those days, so the programmers needed a
crutch to help them remember the types.

In modern languages we have much richer type systems, and the compilers
remember and enforce the types. What’s more, there is a trend toward
smaller classes and shorter functions so that people can usually see the
point of declaration of each variable they’re using.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_027.html_filepos161254"
class="calibre2"></span>Java programmers don’t need type encoding.
Objects are strongly typed, and editing environments have advanced such
that they detect a type error long before you can run a compile! So
nowadays HN and other forms of type encoding are simply impediments.
They make it harder to change the name or type of a variable, function,
or class. They make it harder to read the code. And they create the
possibility that the encoding system will mislead the reader.

<span class="calibre28">   PhoneNumber phoneString;  
   // name not changed when type changed!</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_027.html_filepos161959"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Member Prefixes**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

You also don’t need to prefix member variables with
<span class="calibre28">`m_`</span> anymore. Your classes and functions
should be small enough that you don’t need them. And you should be using
an editing environment that highlights or colorizes members to make them
distinct.

<span class="calibre28">   public class Part {  
     private String m_dsc; // The textual description  
     void setName(String name) {  
       m_dsc = name;  
     }  
   }  
   \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_  
   public class Part {  
     String description;  
     void setDescription(String description) {  
       this.description = description;  
     }  
   }</span>

Besides, people quickly learn to ignore the prefix (or suffix) to see
the meaningful part of the name. The more we read the code, the less we
see the prefixes. Eventually the prefixes become unseen clutter and a
marker of older code.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_027.html_filepos163505"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Interfaces and Implementations**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

These are sometimes a special case for encodings. For example, say you
are building an A<span class="small">BSTRACT</span>
F<span class="small">ACTORY</span> for the creation of shapes. This
factory will be an interface and will be implemented by a concrete
class. What should you name them?
<span class="calibre28">`IShapeFactory`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`ShapeFactory`</span>? I prefer to leave
interfaces unadorned. The preceding <span class="calibre28">`I`</span>,
so common in today’s legacy wads, is a distraction at best and too much
information at worst. I don’t want my users knowing that I’m handing
them an interface. I just want them to know that it’s a
<span class="calibre28">`ShapeFactory`</span>. So if I must encode
either the interface or the implementation, I choose the implementation.
Calling it <span class="calibre28">`ShapeFactoryImp`</span>, or even the
hideous <span class="calibre28">`CShapeFactory`</span>, is preferable to
encoding the interface.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_027.html_filepos164718"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_027.html_calibre_pb_36" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_028.html"></span>

### <span id="Clean_Code_split_028.html_filepos164787" class="calibre10"></span>**Avoid Mental Mapping**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Readers shouldn’t have to mentally translate your names into other names
they already know. This problem generally arises from a choice to use
neither problem domain terms nor solution domain terms.

This is a problem with single-letter variable names. Certainly a loop
counter may be named <span class="calibre28">`i`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`j`</span> or <span class="calibre28">`k`</span>
(though never <span class="calibre28">`l`</span>!) if its scope is very
small and no other names can conflict with it. This is because those
single-letter names for loop counters are traditional. However, in most
other contexts a single-letter name is a poor choice; it’s just a place
holder that the reader must mentally map to the actual concept. There
can be no worse reason for using the name
<span class="calibre28">`c`</span> than because
<span class="calibre28">`a`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`b`</span> were already taken.

In general programmers are pretty smart people. Smart people sometimes
like to show off their smarts by demonstrating their mental juggling
abilities. After all, if you can reliably remember that
<span class="calibre28">`r`</span> is the lower-cased version of the url
with the host and scheme removed, then you must clearly be very smart.

One difference between a smart programmer and a professional programmer
is that the professional understands that *clarity is king*.
Professionals use their powers for good and write code that others can
understand.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_028.html_filepos166644"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_028.html_calibre_pb_37" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_029.html"></span>

### **Class Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Classes and objects should have noun or noun phrase names like
<span class="calibre28">`Customer`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`WikiPage`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`Account`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`AddressParser`</span>. Avoid words like
<span class="calibre28">`Manager`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`Processor`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`Data`</span>, or
<span class="calibre28">`Info`</span> in the name of a class. A class
name should not be a verb.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_029.html_filepos167356"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_029.html_calibre_pb_38" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_030.html"></span>

### **Method Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Methods should have verb or verb phrase names like
<span class="calibre28">`postPayment`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`deletePage`</span>, or
<span class="calibre28">`save`</span>. Accessors, mutators, and
predicates should be named for their value and prefixed with
<span class="calibre28">`get`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`set`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`is`</span> according to the javabean
standard.<sup>4</sup>

<span class="calibre34">4.
<a href="http://java.sun.com/products/javabeans/docs/spec.html"
class="calibre18">http://java.sun.com/products/javabeans/docs/spec.html</a></span>

<span class="calibre28">   string name = employee.getName();  
   customer.setName(”mike”);  
   if (paycheck.isPosted())…</span>

When constructors are overloaded, use static factory methods with names
that describe the arguments. For example,

<span class="calibre28">   Complex fulcrumPoint =
Complex.FromRealNumber(23.0);</span>

is generally better than

<span class="calibre28">   Complex fulcrumPoint = new
Complex(23.0);</span>

Consider enforcing their use by making the corresponding constructors
private.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_030.html_filepos169147"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_030.html_calibre_pb_39" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_031.html"></span>

### <span id="Clean_Code_split_031.html_filepos169216" class="calibre10"></span>**Don’t Be Cute**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

If names are too clever, they will be memorable only to people who share
the author’s sense of humor, and only as long as these people remember
the joke. Will they know what the function named
<span class="calibre28">`HolyHandGrenade`</span> is supposed to do?
Sure, it’s cute, but maybe in this case
<span class="calibre28">`DeleteItems`</span> might be a better name.
Choose clarity over entertainment value.

<img src="media/images/00017.jpg" class="calibre4" alt="Image" />

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Cuteness in code often appears in the form of colloquialisms or slang.
For example, don’t use the name <span class="calibre28">`whack()`</span>
to mean <span class="calibre28">`kill()`</span>. Don’t tell little
culture-dependent jokes like
<span class="calibre28">`eatMyShorts()`</span> to mean
<span class="calibre28">`abort()`</span>.

Say what you mean. Mean what you say.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_031.html_filepos170419"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_031.html_calibre_pb_40" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_032.html"></span>

### **Pick One Word per Concept**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Pick one word for one abstract concept and stick with it. For instance,
it’s confusing to have <span class="calibre28">`fetch`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`retrieve,`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`get`</span> as equivalent methods of different
classes. How do you remember which method name goes with which class?
Sadly, you often have to remember which company, group, or individual
wrote the library or class in order to remember which term was used.
Otherwise, you spend an awful lot of time browsing through headers and
previous code samples.

Modern editing environments like Eclipse and IntelliJ-provide
context-sensitive clues, such as the list of methods you can call on a
given object. But note that the list doesn’t usually give you the
comments you wrote around your function names and parameter lists. You
are lucky if it gives the parameter *names* from function declarations.
The function names have to stand alone, and they have to be consistent
in order for you to pick the correct method without any additional
exploration.

Likewise, it’s confusing to have a
<span class="calibre28">`controller`</span> and a
<span class="calibre28">`manager`</span> and a
<span class="calibre28">`driver`</span> in the same code base. What is
the essential difference between a
<span class="calibre28">`DeviceManager`</span> and a
<span class="calibre28">`Protocol-Controller`</span>? Why are both not
<span class="calibre28">`controllers`</span> or both not
<span class="calibre28">`managers`</span>? Are they both Drivers really?
The name leads you to expect two objects that have very different type
as well as having different classes.

A consistent lexicon is a great boon to the programmers who must use
your code.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_032.html_filepos172587"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_032.html_calibre_pb_41" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_033.html"></span>

### **Don’t Pun**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Avoid using the same word for two purposes. Using the same term for two
different ideas is essentially a pun.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_033.html_filepos172912"
class="calibre2"></span>If you follow the “one word per concept” rule,
you could end up with many classes that have, for example, an
<span class="calibre28">`add`</span> method. As long as the parameter
lists and return values of the various
<span class="calibre28">`add`</span> methods are semantically
equivalent, all is well.

However one might decide to use the word
<span class="calibre28">`add`</span> for “consistency” when he or she is
not in fact adding in the same sense. Let’s say we have many classes
where <span class="calibre28">`add`</span> will create a new value by
adding or concatenating two existing values. Now let’s say we are
writing a new class that has a method that puts its single parameter
into a collection. Should we call this method
<span class="calibre28">`add`</span>? It might seem consistent because
we have so many other <span class="calibre28">`add`</span> methods, but
in this case, the semantics are different, so we should use a name like
<span class="calibre28">`insert`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`append`</span> instead. To call the new method
<span class="calibre28">`add`</span> would be a pun.

Our goal, as authors, is to make our code as easy as possible to
understand. We want our code to be a quick skim, not an intense study.
We want to use the popular paperback model whereby the author is
responsible for making himself clear and not the academic model where it
is the scholar’s job to dig the meaning out of the paper.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_033.html_filepos174601"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_033.html_calibre_pb_42" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_034.html"></span>

### **Use Solution Domain Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Remember that the people who read your code will be programmers. So go
ahead and use computer science (CS) terms, algorithm names, pattern
names, math terms, and so forth. It is not wise to draw every name from
the problem domain because we don’t want our coworkers to have to run
back and forth to the customer asking what every name means when they
already know the concept by a different name.

The name <span class="calibre28">`AccountVisitor`</span> means a great
deal to a programmer who is familiar with the
V<span class="small">ISITOR</span> pattern. What programmer would not
know what a <span class="calibre28">`JobQueue`</span> was? There are
lots of very technical things that programmers have to do. Choosing
technical names for those things is usually the most appropriate course.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_034.html_filepos175674"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_034.html_calibre_pb_43" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_035.html"></span>

### **Use Problem Domain Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

When there is no “programmer-eese” for what you’re doing, use the name
from the problem domain. At least the programmer who maintains your code
can ask a domain expert what it means.

Separating solution and problem domain concepts is part of the job of a
good programmer and designer. The code that has more to do with problem
domain concepts should have names drawn from the problem domain.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_035.html_filepos176364"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_035.html_calibre_pb_44" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_036.html"></span>

### **Add Meaningful Context**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

There are a few names which are meaningful in and of themselves—most are
not. Instead, you need to place names in context for your reader by
enclosing them in well-named classes, functions, or namespaces. When all
else fails, then prefixing the name may be necessary as a last resort.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos176877"
class="calibre2"></span>Imagine that you have variables named
<span class="calibre28">`firstName`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`lastName`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`street`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`houseNumber`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`city`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`state`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`zipcode`</span>. Taken together it’s pretty
clear that they form an address. But what if you just saw the
<span class="calibre28">`state`</span> variable being used alone in a
method? Would you automatically infer that it was part of an address?

You can add context by using prefixes:
<span class="calibre28">`addrFirstName`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`addrLastName`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`addrState`</span>, and so on. At least readers
will understand that these variables are part of a larger structure. Of
course, a better solution is to create a class named
<span class="calibre28">`Address`</span>. Then, even the compiler knows
that the variables belong to a bigger concept.

Consider the method in
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos178683"
class="calibre18">Listing 2-1</a>. Do the variables need a more
meaningful context? The function name provides only part of the context;
the algorithm provides the rest. Once you read through the function, you
see that the three variables, <span class="calibre28">`number`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`verb`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`pluralModifier`</span>, are part of the “guess
statistics” message. Unfortunately, the context must be inferred. When
you first look at the method, the meanings of the variables are opaque.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos178683"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 2-1
<span class="calibre37">`Variables with unclear context`</span>**.</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   private void printGuessStatistics(char
candidate, int count) {   String number;  
       String verb;  
       String pluralModifier;  
       if (count == 0) {  
         number = ”no”;  
         verb = ”are”;  
         pluralModifier = ”s”;  
       } else if (count == 1) {  
         number = ”1”;  
         verb = ”is”;  
         pluralModifier = ””;  
       } else {  
         number = Integer.toString(count);  
         verb = ”are”;  
         pluralModifier = ”s”;  
       }  
       String guessMessage = String.format(  
         ”There %s %s %s%s”, verb, number, candidate, pluralModifier  
       );  
       print(guessMessage);  
     }</span>

The function is a bit too long and the variables are used throughout. To
split the function into smaller pieces we need to create a
<span class="calibre28">`GuessStatisticsMessage`</span> class and make
the three variables fields of this class. This provides a clear context
for the three variables. They are *definitively* part of the
<span class="calibre28">`GuessStatisticsMessage`</span>. The improvement
of context also allows the algorithm to be made much cleaner by breaking
it into many smaller functions. (See
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos181257"
class="calibre18">Listing 2-2</a>.)

<span id="Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos181257"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5"><span id="Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos181336"
class="calibre2"></span>**Listing 2-2
<span class="calibre37">`Variables have a context`</span>.**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public class GuessStatisticsMessage {  
     private String number;  
     private String verb;  
     private String pluralModifier;  
     
     public String make(char candidate, int count) {  
       createPluralDependentMessageParts(count);  
        return String.format(  
          "There %s %s %s%s",  
          verb, number, candidate, pluralModifier );  
     }  
     
     private void createPluralDependentMessageParts(int count) {  
       if (count == 0) {  
         thereAreNoLetters();  
       } else if (count == 1) {  
         thereIsOneLetter();  
       } else {  
         thereAreManyLetters(count);  
       }  
     }  
     
     private void thereAreManyLetters(int count) {  
       number = Integer.toString(count);  
       verb = "are";  
       pluralModifier = "s";  
     }  
     
     private void thereIsOneLetter() {  
       number = "1";  
       verb = "is";  
       pluralModifier = "";  
     }  
     
     private void thereAreNoLetters() {  
       number = "no";  
       verb = "are";  
       pluralModifier = "s";  
     }  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_036.html_filepos183937"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_036.html_calibre_pb_45" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_037.html"></span>

### **Don’t Add Gratuitous Context**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In an imaginary application called “Gas Station Deluxe,” it is a bad
idea to prefix every class with <span class="calibre28">`GSD`</span>.
Frankly, you are working against your tools. You type
<span class="calibre28">`G`</span> and press the completion key and are
rewarded with a mile-long list of every class in the system. Is that
wise? Why make it hard for the IDE to help you?

Likewise, say you invented a
<span class="calibre28">`MailingAddress`</span> class in
<span class="calibre28">`GSD`</span>’s accounting module, and you named
it <span class="calibre28">`GSDAccountAddress`</span>. Later, you need a
mailing address for your customer contact application. Do you use
<span class="calibre28">`GSDAccountAddress`</span>? Does it sound like
the right name? Ten of 17 characters are redundant or irrelevant.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_037.html_filepos185073"
class="calibre2"></span>Shorter names are generally better than longer
ones, so long as they are clear. Add no more context to a name than is
necessary.

The names <span class="calibre28">`accountAddress`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`customerAddress`</span> are fine names for
instances of the class <span class="calibre28">`Address`</span> but
could be poor names for classes.
<span class="calibre28">`Address`</span> is a fine name for a class. If
I need to differentiate between MAC addresses, port addresses, and Web
addresses, I might consider
<span class="calibre28">`PostalAddress`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`MAC`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`URI`</span>. The resulting names are more
precise, which is the point of all naming.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_037.html_filepos185937"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_037.html_calibre_pb_46" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_038.html"></span>

### **Final Words**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The hardest thing about choosing good names is that it requires good
descriptive skills and a shared cultural background. This is a teaching
issue rather than a technical, business, or management issue. As a
result many people in this field don’t learn to do it very well.

People are also afraid of renaming things for fear that some other
developers will object. We do not share that fear and find that we are
actually grateful when names change (for the better). Most of the time
we don’t really memorize the names of classes and methods. We use the
modern tools to deal with details like that so we can focus on whether
the code reads like paragraphs and sentences, or at least like tables
and data structure (a sentence isn’t always the best way to display
data). You will probably end up surprising someone when you rename, just
like you might with any other code improvement. Don’t let it stop you in
your tracks.

Follow some of these rules and see whether you don’t improve the
readability of your code. If you are maintaining someone else’s code,
use refactoring tools to help resolve these problems. It will pay off in
the short term and continue to pay in the long run.

<div id="Clean_Code_split_038.html_calibre_pb_47" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_039.html"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos187493" style="height:0pt">

</div>

## <span class="calibre10"></span>**3 Functions**

<div class="calibre21">

 

</div>

<img src="media/images/00018.jpg" class="calibre4" alt="Image" />

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In the early days of programming we composed our systems of routines and
subroutines. Then, in the era of Fortran and PL/1 we composed our
systems of programs, subprograms, and functions. Nowadays only the
function survives from those early days. Functions are the first line of
organization in any program. Writing them well is the topic of this
chapter.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188183"
class="calibre2"></span>Consider the code in
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-1</a>. It’s hard to find a long function in
FitNesse,<sup>1</sup> but after a bit of searching I came across this
one. Not only is it long, but it’s got duplicated code, lots of odd
strings, and many strange and inobvious data types and APIs. See how
much sense you can make of it in the next three minutes.

<span class="calibre34">1. An open-source testing tool.
<a href="http://www.fitnese.org" class="calibre18">www.fitnese.org</a></span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 3-1
<span class="calibre37">`HtmlUtil.java (FitNesse 20070619)`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public static String testableHtml(  
     PageData pageData,  
     boolean includeSuiteSetup  
   ) throws Exception {  
     WikiPage wikiPage = pageData.getWikiPage();  
     StringBuffer buffer = new StringBuffer();  
     if (pageData.hasAttribute("Test")) {  
       if (includeSuiteSetup) {  
         WikiPage suiteSetup =  
          PageCrawlerImpl.getInheritedPage(  
                  SuiteResponder.SUITE_SETUP_NAME, wikiPage  
          );  
         if (suiteSetup != null) {  
          WikiPagePath pagePath =  
            suiteSetup.getPageCrawler().getFullPath(suiteSetup);  
          String pagePathName = PathParser.render(pagePath);  
          buffer.append("!include -setup .")  
                .append(pagePathName)  
                .append("\n");  
         }  
       }  
       WikiPage setup =  
         PageCrawlerImpl.getInheritedPage("SetUp", wikiPage);  
       if (setup != null) {  
         WikiPagePath setupPath =  
           wikiPage.getPageCrawler().getFullPath(setup);  
         String setupPathName = PathParser.render(setupPath);  
         buffer.append("!include -setup .")  
               .append(setupPathName)  
               .append("\n");  
       }  
     }  
     buffer.append(pageData.getContent());  
     if (pageData.hasAttribute("Test")) {  
       WikiPage teardown =  
         PageCrawlerImpl.getInheritedPage("TearDown", wikiPage);  
       if (teardown != null) {  
         WikiPagePath tearDownPath =  
           wikiPage.getPageCrawler().getFullPath(teardown);  
         String tearDownPathName = PathParser.render(tearDownPath);  
         buffer.append("\n")  
               .append("!include -teardown .")  
               .append(tearDownPathName)  
               .append("\n");  
       }<span class="calibre2"></span>  
        if (includeSuiteSetup) {  
          WikiPage suiteTeardown =  
            PageCrawlerImpl.getInheritedPage(  
                    SuiteResponder.SUITE_TEARDOWN_NAME,  
                    wikiPage  
            );  
          if (suiteTeardown != null) {  
            WikiPagePath pagePath =  
              suiteTeardown.getPageCrawler().getFullPath
(suiteTeardown);  
            String pagePathName = PathParser.render(pagePath);  
            buffer.append("!include -teardown .")  
                  .append(pagePathName)  
                  .append("\n");  
          }  
        }  
      }  
      pageData.setContent(buffer.toString());  
      return pageData.getHtml();  
   }</span>

Do you understand the function after three minutes of study? Probably
not. There’s too much going on in there at too many different levels of
abstraction. There are strange strings and odd function calls mixed in
with doubly nested <span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statements
controlled by flags.

However, with just a few simple method extractions, some renaming, and a
little restructuring, I was able to capture the intent of the function
in the nine lines of <a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-2</a>. See whether you can understand *that*
in the next 3 minutes.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 3-2
<span class="calibre37">`HtmlUtil.java (refactored)`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public static String
renderPageWithSetupsAndTeardowns(  
     PageData pageData, boolean isSuite  
   ) throws Exception {  
     boolean isTestPage = pageData.hasAttribute("Test");  
     if (isTestPage) {  
       WikiPage testPage = pageData.getWikiPage();  
       StringBuffer newPageContent = new StringBuffer();  
       includeSetupPages(testPage, newPageContent, isSuite);  
       newPageContent.append(pageData.getContent());  
       includeTeardownPages(testPage, newPageContent, isSuite);  
       pageData.setContent(newPageContent.toString());  
     }  
      
     return pageData.getHtml();  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos196913"
class="calibre2"></span>Unless you are a student of FitNesse, you
probably don’t understand all the details. Still, you probably
understand that this function performs the inclusion of some setup and
teardown pages into a test page and then renders that page into HTML. If
you are familiar with JUnit,<sup>2</sup> you probably realize that this
function belongs to some kind of Web-based testing framework. And, of
course, that is correct. Divining that information from
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-2</a> is pretty easy, but it’s pretty well
obscured by <a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-1</a>.

<span class="calibre34">2. An open-source unit-testing tool for Java.
<a href="http://www.junit.org" class="calibre18">www.junit.org</a></span>

So what is it that makes a function like
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-2</a> easy to read and understand? How can
we make a function communicate its intent? What attributes can we give
our functions that will allow a casual reader to intuit the kind of
program they live inside?

<span id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos198087"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_039.html_calibre_pb_50" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_040.html"></span>

### **Small!**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The first rule of functions is that they should be small. The second
rule of functions is that *they should be smaller than that*. This is
not an assertion that I can justify. I can’t provide any references to
research that shows that very small functions are better. What I can
tell you is that for nearly four decades I have written functions of all
different sizes. I’ve written several nasty 3,000-line abominations.
I’ve written scads of functions in the 100 to 300 line range. And I’ve
written functions that were 20 to 30 lines long. What this experience
has taught me, through long trial and error, is that functions should be
very small.

In the eighties we used to say that a function should be no bigger than
a screen-full. Of course we said that at a time when VT100 screens were
24 lines by 80 columns, and our editors used 4 lines for administrative
purposes. Nowadays with a cranked-down font and a nice big monitor, you
can fit 150 characters on a line and a 100 lines or more on a screen.
Lines should not be 150 characters long. Functions should not be 100
lines long. Functions should hardly ever be 20 lines long.

How short should a function be? In 1999 I went to visit Kent Beck at his
home in Oregon. We sat down and did some programming together. At one
point he showed me a cute little Java/Swing program that he called
*Sparkle*. It produced a visual effect on the screen very similar to the
magic wand of the fairy godmother in the movie Cinderella. As you moved
the mouse, the sparkles would drip from the cursor with a satisfying
scintillation, falling to the bottom of the window through a simulated
gravitational field. When Kent showed me the code, I was struck by how
small all the functions were. I was used to functions in Swing programs
that took up miles of vertical space. Every function in *this* program
was just two, or three, or four lines long. Each was transparently
obvious. Each told a story. And each led you to the next in a compelling
order. *That’s* how short your functions should be!<sup>3</sup>

<span class="calibre34">3. I asked Kent whether he still had a copy, but
he was unable to find one. I searched all my old computers too, but to
no avail. All that is left now is my memory of that program.</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos200768"
class="calibre2"></span>How short should your functions be? They should
usually be shorter than
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-2</a>! Indeed,
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-2</a> should really be shortened to
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos201060"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-3</a>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos201060"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 3-3
<span class="calibre37">`HtmlUtil.java (re-refactored)`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public static String renderPageWith  
       SetupsAndTeardowns(  
   PageData pageData, boolean isSuite) throws Exception {  
     if (isTestPage(pageData))  
       includeSetupAndTeardownPages(pageData, isSuite);  
     return pageData.getHtml();  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos201811"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Blocks and Indenting**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

This implies that the blocks within <span class="calibre28">`if`</span>
statements, <span class="calibre28">`else`</span> statements,
<span class="calibre28">`while`</span> statements, and so on should be
one line long. Probably that line should be a function call. Not only
does this keep the enclosing function small, but it also adds
documentary value because the function called within the block can have
a nicely descriptive name.

This also implies that functions should not be large enough to hold
nested structures. Therefore, the indent level of a function should not
be greater than one or two. This, of course, makes the functions easier
to read and understand.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos202749"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_040.html_calibre_pb_51" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_041.html"></span>

### **Do One Thing**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It should be very clear that
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-1</a> is doing lots more than one thing.
It’s creating buffers, fetching pages, searching for inherited pages,
rendering paths, appending arcane strings, and generating HTML, among
other things. <a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-1</a> is very busy doing lots of different
things. On the other hand,
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos201060"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-3</a> is doing one simple thing. It’s
including setups and teardowns into test pages.

The following advice has appeared in one form or another for 30 years or
more.

<img src="media/images/00019.jpg" class="calibre4" alt="Image" />

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

> ***F<span class="small">UNCTIONS SHOULD DO ONE THING</span>.
> T<span class="small">HEY SHOULD DO IT WELL</span>.
> T<span class="small">HEY SHOULD DO IT ONLY</span>.***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The problem with this statement is that it is hard to know what “one
thing” is. Does <a href="#Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos201060"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-3</a> do one thing? It’s easy to make the
case that it’s doing three things:

> **1.** Determining whether the page is a test page.

> **2.** If so, including setups and teardowns.

> **3.** Rendering the page in HTML.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_041.html_filepos204648"
class="calibre2"></span>So which is it? Is the function doing one thing
or three things? Notice that the three steps of the function are one
level of abstraction below the stated name of the function. We can
describe the function by describing it as a brief *TO*<sup>4</sup>
paragraph:

<span class="calibre34">4. The LOGO language used the keyword “TO” in
the same way that Ruby and Python use “def.” So every function began
with the word “TO.” This had an interesting effect on the way functions
were designed.</span>

> *TO RenderPageWithSetupsAndTeardowns, we check to see whether the page
> is a test page and if so, we include the setups and teardowns. In
> either case we render the page in HTML.*

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

If a function does only those steps that are one level below the stated
name of the function, then the function is doing one thing. After all,
the reason we write functions is to decompose a larger concept (in other
words, the name of the function) into a set of steps at the next level
of abstraction.

It should be very clear that
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-1</a> contains steps at many different
levels of abstraction. So it is clearly doing more than one thing. Even
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos195554"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-2</a> has two levels of abstraction, as
proved by our ability to shrink it down. But it would be very hard to
meaningfully shrink <a href="#Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos201060"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-3</a>. We could extract the
<span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statement into a function named
<span class="calibre28">`includeSetupsAndTeardownsIfTestPage`</span>,
but that simply restates the code without changing the level of
abstraction.

So, another way to know that a function is doing more than “one thing”
is if you can extract another function from it with a name that is not
merely a restatement of its implementation \[G34\].

<span id="Clean_Code_split_041.html_filepos206865"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Sections within Functions**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Look at <a href="#Clean_Code_split_059.html_filepos295066"
class="calibre18">Listing 4-7</a> on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_059.html_filepos331763"
class="calibre18">71</a>. Notice that the
<span class="calibre28">`generatePrimes`</span> function is divided into
sections such as *declarations*, *initializations*, and *sieve*. This is
an obvious symptom of doing more than one thing. Functions that do one
thing cannot be reasonably divided into sections.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_041.html_filepos207466"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_041.html_calibre_pb_52" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_042.html"></span>

### **One Level of Abstraction per Function**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In order to make sure our functions are doing “one thing,” we need to
make sure that the statements within our function are all at the same
level of abstraction. It is easy to see how
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_039.html_filepos188796"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-1</a> violates this rule. There are concepts
in there that are at a very high level of abstraction, such as
<span class="calibre28">`getHtml()`</span>; others that are at an
intermediate level of abstraction, such as:
<span class="calibre28">`String pagePathName = PathParser.render(pagePath)`</span>;
and still others that are remarkably low level, such as:
<span class="calibre28">`.append(”\n”)`</span>.

Mixing levels of abstraction within a function is always confusing.
Readers may not be able to tell whether a particular expression is an
essential concept or a detail. Worse,
<span id="Clean_Code_split_042.html_filepos208533"
class="calibre2"></span>like broken windows, once details are mixed with
essential concepts, more and more details tend to accrete within the
function.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_042.html_filepos208724"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Reading Code from Top to Bottom: *The Stepdown Rule***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

We want the code to read like a top-down narrative.<sup>5</sup> We want
every function to be followed by those at the next level of abstraction
so that we can read the program, descending one level of abstraction at
a time as we read down the list of functions. I call this *The Step-down
Rule*.

<span class="calibre34">5.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_042.html_filepos209289"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_054.html_filepos267396"
class="calibre18">KP78</a>\], p.
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_042.html_filepos208533"
class="calibre18">37</a>.</span>

To say this differently, we want to be able to read the program as
though it were a set of *TO* paragraphs, each of which is describing the
current level of abstraction and referencing subsequent *TO* paragraphs
at the next level down.

> *To include the setups and teardowns, we include setups, then we
> include the test page content, and then we include the teardowns.*

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

> *To include the setups, we include the suite setup if this is a suite,
> then we include the regular setup.*

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

> *To include the suite setup, we search the parent hierarchy for the
> “SuiteSetUp” page and add an include statement with the path of that
> page.*

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

> *To search the parent…*

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It turns out to be very difficult for programmers to learn to follow
this rule and write functions that stay at a single level of
abstraction. But learning this trick is also very important. It is the
key to keeping functions short and making sure they do “one thing.”
Making the code read like a top-down set of *TO* paragraphs is an
effective technique for keeping the abstraction level consistent.

Take a look at <a href="#Clean_Code_split_053.html_filepos260188"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-7</a> at the end of this chapter. It shows
the whole <span class="calibre28">`testableHtml`</span> function
refactored according to the principles described here. Notice how each
function introduces the next, and each function remains at a consistent
level of abstraction.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_042.html_filepos211413"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_042.html_calibre_pb_53" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html"></span>

### **Switch Statements**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It’s hard to make a small <span class="calibre28">`switch`</span>
statement.<sup>6</sup> Even a <span class="calibre28">`switch`</span>
statement with only two cases is larger than I’d like a single block or
function to be. It’s also hard to make a
<span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statement that does one thing.
By their nature, <span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statements
always do *N* things. Unfortunately we can’t always avoid
<span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statements, but we *can* make
sure that each <span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statement is
buried in a low-level class and is never repeated. We do this, of
course, with polymorphism.

<span class="calibre34">6. And, of course, I include if/else chains in
this.</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos212488"
class="calibre2"></span>Consider
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos212679"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-4</a>. It shows just one of the operations
that might depend on the type of employee.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos212679"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 3-4
<span class="calibre37">`Payroll.java`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public Money calculatePay(Employee e)  
   throws InvalidEmployeeType {  
       switch (e.type) {  
         case COMMISSIONED:  
           return calculateCommissionedPay(e);  
         case HOURLY:  
           return calculateHourlyPay(e);  
         case SALARIED:  
           return calculateSalariedPay(e);  
         default:  
           throw new InvalidEmployeeType(e.type);  
       }  
     }</span>

There are several problems with this function. First, it’s large, and
when new employee types are added, it will grow. Second, it very clearly
does more than one thing. Third, it violates the Single Responsibility
Principle<sup>7</sup> (SRP) because there is more than one reason for it
to change. Fourth, it violates the Open Closed Principle<sup>8</sup>
(OCP) because it must change whenever new types are added. But possibly
the worst problem with this function is that there are an unlimited
number of other functions that will have the same structure. For example
we could have

<span class="calibre34">7. a.
<a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_responsibility_principle"
class="calibre18">http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_responsibility_principle</a></span>

> <span class="calibre34">b.
> <a href="http://www.objectmentor.com/resources/articles/srp.pdf"
> class="calibre18">http://www.objectmentor.com/resources/articles/srp.pdf</a></span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre34">8. a.
<a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open/closed_principle"
class="calibre18">http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open/closed_principle</a></span>

> <span class="calibre34">b.
> <a href="http://www.objectmentor.com/resources/articles/ocp.pdf"
> class="calibre18">http://www.objectmentor.com/resources/articles/ocp.pdf</a></span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   isPayday(Employee e, Date date),</span>

or

<span class="calibre28">   deliverPay(Employee e, Money pay),</span>

or a host of others. All of which would have the same deleterious
structure.

The solution to this problem (see
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos217353"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-5</a>) is to bury the
<span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statement in the basement of an
A<span class="small">BSTRACT</span>
F<span class="small">ACTORY</span>,<sup>9</sup> and never let anyone see
it. The factory will use the <span class="calibre28">`switch`</span>
statement to create appropriate instances of the derivatives of
<span class="calibre28">`Employee`</span>, and the various functions,
such as <span class="calibre28">`calculatePay`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`isPayday`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`deliverPay`</span>, will be dispatched
polymorphically through the <span class="calibre28">`Employee`</span>
interface.

<span class="calibre34">9.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos216775"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_054.html_filepos267950"
class="calibre18">GOF</a>\].</span>

My general rule for <span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statements
is that they can be tolerated if they appear only once, are used to
create polymorphic objects, and are hidden behind an inheritance
<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos217084"
class="calibre2"></span>relationship so that the rest of the system
can’t see them \[<a href="#Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1117244"
class="calibre18">G23</a>\]. Of course every circumstance is unique, and
there are times when I violate one or more parts of that rule.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos217353"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 3-5
<span class="calibre37">`Employee and Factory`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public abstract class Employee {  
     public abstract boolean isPayday();  
     public abstract Money calculatePay();  
     public abstract void deliverPay(Money pay);  
   }  
   -----------------  
   public interface EmployeeFactory {  
     public Employee makeEmployee(EmployeeRecord r) throws
InvalidEmployeeType;  
   }  
   -----------------  
   public class EmployeeFactoryImpl implements  
          EmployeeFactory {  
     public Employee makeEmployee(EmployeeRecord r) throws
InvalidEmployeeType {  
       switch (r.type) {  
         case COMMISSIONED:  
           return new CommissionedEmployee(r) ;  
         case HOURLY:  
           return new HourlyEmployee(r);  
         case SALARIED:  
           return new SalariedEmploye(r);  
         default:  
           throw new InvalidEmployeeType(r.type);  
       }  
     }  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_filepos219358"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_043.html_calibre_pb_54" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_044.html"></span>

### **Use Descriptive Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In <a href="#Clean_Code_split_053.html_filepos260188"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-7</a> I changed the name of our example
function from <span class="calibre28">`testableHtml`</span> to
<span class="calibre28">`SetupTeardownIncluder.render`</span>. This is a
far better name because it better describes what the function does. I
also gave each of the private methods an equally descriptive name such
as <span class="calibre28">`isTestable`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`includeSetupAndTeardownPages`</span>. It is
hard to overestimate the value of good names. Remember Ward’s principle:
“*You know you are working on clean code when each routine turns out to
be pretty much what you expected.*” Half the battle to achieving that
principle is choosing good names for small functions that do one thing.
The smaller and more focused a function is, the easier it is to choose a
descriptive name.

Don’t be afraid to make a name long. A long descriptive name is better
than a short enigmatic name. A long descriptive name is better than a
long descriptive comment. Use a naming convention that allows multiple
words to be easily read in the function names, and then make use of
those multiple words to give the function a name that says what it does.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_044.html_filepos220877"
class="calibre2"></span>Don’t be afraid to spend time choosing a name.
Indeed, you should try several different names and read the code with
each in place. Modern IDEs like Eclipse or IntelliJ make it trivial to
change names. Use one of those IDEs and experiment with different names
until you find one that is as descriptive as you can make it.

Choosing descriptive names will clarify the design of the module in your
mind and help you to improve it. It is not at all uncommon that hunting
for a good name results in a favorable restructuring of the code.

Be consistent in your names. Use the same phrases, nouns, and verbs in
the function names you choose for your modules. Consider, for example,
the names
<span class="calibre28">`includeSetup-AndTeardownPages`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`includeSetupPages`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`includeSuiteSetupPage`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`includeSetupPage`</span>. The similar
phraseology in those names allows the sequence to tell a story. Indeed,
if I showed you just the sequence above, you’d ask yourself: “What
happened to <span class="calibre28">`includeTeardownPages`</span>,
<span class="calibre28">`includeSuiteTeardownPage`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`includeTeardownPage`</span>?” How’s that for
being “… *pretty much what you expected*.”

<span id="Clean_Code_split_044.html_filepos222427"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_044.html_calibre_pb_55" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html"></span>

### **Function Arguments**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The ideal number of arguments for a function is zero (niladic). Next
comes one (monadic), followed closely by two (dyadic). Three arguments
(triadic) should be avoided where possible. More than three (polyadic)
requires very special justification—and then shouldn’t be used anyway.

<img src="media/images/00020.jpg" class="calibre4" alt="Image" />

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Arguments are hard. They take a lot of conceptual power. That’s why I
got rid of almost all of them from the example. Consider, for instance,
the <span class="calibre28">`StringBuffer`</span> in the example. We
could have passed it around as an argument rather than making it an
instance variable, but then our readers would have had to interpret it
each time they saw it. When you are reading the story told by the
module, <span class="calibre28">`includeSetupPage()`</span> is easier to
understand than
<span class="calibre28">`includeSetupPageInto(newPage-Content)`</span>.
The argument is at a different level of abstraction than the function
name and forces you to know a detail (in other words,
<span class="calibre28">`StringBuffer`</span>) that isn’t particularly
important at that point.

Arguments are even harder from a testing point of view. Imagine the
difficulty of writing all the test cases to ensure that all the various
combinations of arguments work properly. If there are no arguments, this
is trivial. If there’s one argument, it’s not too hard. With two
arguments the problem gets a bit more challenging. With more than two
arguments, testing every combination of appropriate values can be
daunting.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos224416"
class="calibre2"></span>Output arguments are harder to understand than
input arguments. When we read a function, we are used to the idea of
information going *in* to the function through arguments and *out*
through the return value. We don’t usually expect information to be
going out through the arguments. So output arguments often cause us to
do a double-take.

One input argument is the next best thing to no arguments.
<span class="calibre28">`SetupTeardown-Includer.render(pageData)`</span>
is pretty easy to understand. Clearly we are going to *render* the data
in the <span class="calibre28">`pageData`</span> object.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos225167"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Common Monadic Forms**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

There are two very common reasons to pass a single argument into a
function. You may be asking a question about that argument, as in
<span class="calibre28">`boolean`</span>
<span class="calibre28">`fileExists(“MyFile”)`</span>. Or you may be
operating on that argument, transforming it into something else and
*returning it*. For example,
<span class="calibre28">`InputStream`</span>
<span class="calibre28">`fileOpen(“MyFile”)`</span> transforms a file
name <span class="calibre28">`String`</span> into an
<span class="calibre28">`InputStream`</span> return value. These two
uses are what readers expect when they see a function. You should choose
names that make the distinction clear, and always use the two forms in a
consistent context. (See
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos242919"
class="calibre18">Command Query Separation</a> below.)

A somewhat less common, but still very useful form for a single argument
function, is an *event*. In this form there is an input argument but no
output argument. The overall program is meant to interpret the function
call as an event and use the argument to alter the state of the system,
for example,
<span class="calibre28">`void passwordAttemptFailedNtimes(int attempts)`</span>.
Use this form with care. It should be very clear to the reader that this
is an event. Choose names and contexts carefully.

Try to avoid any monadic functions that don’t follow these forms, for
example,
<span class="calibre28">`void includeSetupPageInto(StringBuffer pageText)`</span>.
Using an output argument instead of a return value for a transformation
is confusing. If a function is going to transform its input argument,
the transformation should appear as the return value. Indeed,
<span class="calibre28">`StringBuffer transform(StringBuffer in)`</span>
is better than
<span class="calibre28">`void transform-(StringBuffer out)`</span>, even
if the implementation in the first case simply returns the input
argument. At least it still follows the form of a transformation.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos227519"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Flag Arguments**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Flag arguments are ugly. Passing a boolean into a function is a truly
terrible practice. It immediately complicates the signature of the
method, loudly proclaiming that this function does more than one thing.
It does one thing if the flag is true and another if the flag is false!

In <a href="#Clean_Code_split_053.html_filepos260188"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-7</a> we had no choice because the callers
were already passing that flag in, and I wanted to limit the scope of
refactoring to the function and below. Still, the method call
<span class="calibre28">`render(true)`</span> is just plain confusing to
a poor reader. Mousing over the call and seeing
<span class="calibre28">`render(boolean isSuite)`</span> helps a little,
but not that much. We should have split the function into two:
<span class="calibre28">`renderForSuite()`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`renderForSingleTest()`</span>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos228640"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos228693" class="calibre2"></span>**Dyadic Functions**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

A function with two arguments is harder to understand than a monadic
function. For example, <span class="calibre28">`writeField(name)`</span>
is easier to understand than
<span class="calibre28">`writeField(output-Stream, name)`</span>.<sup>10</sup>
Though the meaning of both is clear, the first glides past the eye,
easily depositing its meaning. The second requires a short pause until
we learn to ignore the first parameter. And *that*, of course,
eventually results in problems because we should never ignore any part
of code. The parts we ignore are where the bugs will hide.

<span class="calibre34">10. I just finished refactoring a module that
used the dyadic form. I was able to make the
<span class="calibre28">`outputStream`</span> a field of the class and
convert all the <span class="calibre28">`writeField`</span> calls to the
monadic form. The result was much cleaner.</span>

There are times, of course, where two arguments are appropriate. For
example, <span class="calibre28">`Point p = new Point(0,0);`</span> is
perfectly reasonable. Cartesian points naturally take two arguments.
Indeed, we’d be very surprised to see
<span class="calibre28">`new Point(0)`</span>. However, the two
arguments in this case *are ordered components of a single value!*
Whereas <span class="calibre28">`output-Stream`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`name`</span> have neither a natural cohesion,
nor a natural ordering.

Even obvious dyadic functions like
<span class="calibre28">`assertEquals(expected, actual)`</span> are
problematic. How many times have you put the
<span class="calibre28">`actual`</span> where the
<span class="calibre28">`expected`</span> should be? The two arguments
have no natural ordering. The <span class="calibre28">`expected,`</span>
<span class="calibre28">`actual`</span> ordering is a convention that
requires practice to learn.

Dyads aren’t evil, and you will certainly have to write them. However,
you should be aware that they come at a cost and should take advantage
of what mechanims may be available to you to convert them into monads.
For example, you might make the
<span class="calibre28">`writeField`</span> method a member of
<span class="calibre28">`outputStream`</span> so that you can say
<span class="calibre28">`outputStream. writeField(name)`</span>. Or you
might make the <span class="calibre28">`outputStream`</span> a member
variable of the current class so that you don’t have to pass it. Or you
might extract a new class like
<span class="calibre28">`FieldWriter`</span> that takes the
<span class="calibre28">`outputStream`</span> in its constructor and has
a <span class="calibre28">`write`</span> method.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos231798"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Triads**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Functions that take three arguments are significantly harder to
understand than dyads. The issues of ordering, pausing, and ignoring are
more than doubled. I suggest you think very carefully before creating a
triad.

For example, consider the common overload of
<span class="calibre28">`assertEquals`</span> that takes three
arguments:
<span class="calibre28">`assertEquals(message, expected, actual)`</span>.
How many times have you read the
<span class="calibre28">`message`</span> and thought it was the
<span class="calibre28">`expected`</span>? I have stumbled and paused
over that particular triad many times. In fact, *every time I see it,* I
do a double-take and then learn to ignore the message.

On the other hand, here is a triad that is not quite so insidious:
<span class="calibre28">`assertEquals(1.0, amount, .001)`</span>.
Although this still requires a double-take, it’s one that’s worth
taking. It’s always good to be reminded that equality of floating point
values is a relative thing.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos233138"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos233191" class="calibre2"></span>**Argument Objects**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

When a function seems to need more than two or three arguments, it is
likely that some of those arguments ought to be wrapped into a class of
their own. Consider, for example, the difference between the two
following declarations:

<span class="calibre28">   Circle makeCircle(double x, double y, double
radius);  
   Circle makeCircle(Point center, double radius);</span>

Reducing the number of arguments by creating objects out of them may
seem like cheating, but it’s not. When groups of variables are passed
together, the way <span class="calibre28">`x`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`y`</span> are in the example above, they are
likely part of a concept that deserves a name of its own.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos234186"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Argument Lists**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Sometimes we want to pass a variable number of arguments into a
function. Consider, for example, the
<span class="calibre28">`String.format`</span> method:

<span class="calibre28">   String.format(”%s worked %.2f hours.”, name,
hours);</span>

If the variable arguments are all treated identically, as they are in
the example above, then they are equivalent to a single argument of type
<span class="calibre28">`List`</span>. By that reasoning,
<span class="calibre28">`String.format`</span> is actually dyadic.
Indeed, the declaration of
<span class="calibre28">`String.format`</span> as shown below is clearly
dyadic.

<span class="calibre28">   public String format(String format, Object…
args)</span>

So all the same rules apply. Functions that take variable arguments can
be monads, dyads, or even triads. But it would be a mistake to give them
more arguments than that.

<span class="calibre28">   void monad(Integer… args);  
   void dyad(String name, Integer… args);  
   void triad(String name, int count, Integer… args);</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos235795"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **Verbs and Keywords**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Choosing good names for a function can go a long way toward explaining
the intent of the function and the order and intent of the arguments. In
the case of a monad, the function and argument should form a very nice
verb/noun pair. For example,
<span class="calibre28">`write(name)`</span> is very evocative. Whatever
this “name” thing is, it is being “written.” An even better name might
be <span class="calibre28">`writeField(name)`</span>, which tells us
that the “name” thing is a “field.”

This last is an example of the *keyword* form of a function name. Using
this form we encode the names of the arguments into the function name.
For example, <span class="calibre28">`assertEquals`</span> might be
better written as
<span class="calibre28">`assertExpectedEqualsActual(expected,`</span>
<span class="calibre28">`actual)`</span>. This strongly mitigates the
problem of having to remember the ordering of the arguments.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos237041"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_045.html_calibre_pb_56" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_046.html"></span>

### <span id="Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos237110" class="calibre10"></span>**Have No Side Effects**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Side effects are lies. Your function promises to do one thing, but it
also does other *hidden* things. Sometimes it will make unexpected
changes to the variables of its own class. Sometimes it will make them
to the parameters passed into the function or to system globals. In
either case they are devious and damaging mistruths that often result in
strange temporal couplings and order dependencies.

Consider, for example, the seemingly innocuous function in
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos238157"
class="calibre18">Listing 3-6</a>. This function uses a standard
algorithm to match a <span class="calibre28">`userName`</span> to a
<span class="calibre28">`password`</span>. It returns
<span class="calibre28">`true`</span> if they match and
<span class="calibre28">`false`</span> if anything goes wrong. But it
also has a side effect. Can you spot it?

<span id="Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos238157"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 3-6
<span class="calibre37">`UserValidator.java`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public class UserValidator {  
     private Cryptographer cryptographer;  
  
     public boolean checkPassword(String userName, String password) {  
       User user = UserGateway.findByName(userName);  
       if (user != User.NULL) {  
         String codedPhrase = user.  
         getPhraseEncodedByPassword();  
         String phrase = cryptographer.decrypt(codedPhrase, password);  
         if ("Valid Password".equals(phrase)) {  
           Session.initialize();  
           return true;  
         }  
       }  
       return false;  
     }  
   }</span>

The side effect is the call to
<span class="calibre28">`Session.initialize()`</span>, of course. The
<span class="calibre28">`checkPassword`</span> function, by its name,
says that it checks the password. The name does not imply that it
initializes the session. So a caller who believes what the name of the
function says runs the risk of erasing the existing session data when he
or she decides to check the validity of the user.

This side effect creates a temporal coupling. That is,
<span class="calibre28">`checkPassword`</span> can only be called at
certain times (in other words, when it is safe to initialize the
session). If it is called out of order, session data may be
inadvertently lost. Temporal couplings are confusing, especially when
hidden as a side effect. If you must have a temporal coupling, you
should make it clear in the name of the function. In this case we might
rename the function
<span class="calibre28">`checkPasswordAndInitializeSession`</span>,
though that certainly violates “Do one thing.”

<span id="Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos240794"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos240847" class="calibre2"></span>**Output Arguments**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Arguments are most naturally interpreted as *inputs* to a function. If
you have been programming for more than a few years, I’m sure you’ve
done a double-take on an argument that was actually an *output* rather
than an input. For example:

<span class="calibre28">   appendFooter(s);</span>

Does this function append <span class="calibre28">`s`</span> as the
footer to something? Or does it append some footer to
<span class="calibre28">`s`</span>? Is
<span class="calibre28">`s`</span> an input or an output? It doesn’t
take long to look at the function signature and see:

<span class="calibre28">   public void appendFooter(StringBuffer
report)</span>

This clarifies the issue, but only at the expense of checking the
declaration of the function. Anything that forces you to check the
function signature is equivalent to a double-take. It’s a cognitive
break and should be avoided.

In the days before object oriented programming it was sometimes
necessary to have output arguments. However, much of the need for output
arguments disappears in OO languages because
<span class="calibre28">`this`</span> is *intended* to act as an output
argument. In other words, it would be better for
<span class="calibre28">`appendFooter`</span> to be invoked as

<span class="calibre28">   report.appendFooter();</span>

In general output arguments should be avoided. If your function must
change the state of something, have it change the state of its owning
object.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos242919"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_046.html_calibre_pb_57" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_047.html"></span>

### **Command Query Separation**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Functions should either do something or answer something, but not both.
Either your function should change the state of an object, or it should
return some information about that object. Doing both often leads to
confusion. Consider, for example, the following function:

<span class="calibre28">   public boolean set(String attribute, String
value);</span>

This function sets the value of a named attribute and returns
<span class="calibre28">`true`</span> if it is successful and
<span class="calibre28">`false`</span> if no such attribute exists. This
leads to odd statements like this:

<span class="calibre28">   if (set(”username”, ”unclebob”))…</span>

Imagine this from the point of view of the reader. What does it mean? Is
it asking whether the “<span class="calibre28">`username`</span>”
attribute was previously set to
“<span class="calibre28">`unclebob`</span>”? Or is it asking whether the
“<span class="calibre28">`username`</span>” attribute was successfully
set to “<span class="calibre28">`unclebob`</span>”? It’s hard to infer
the meaning from the call because it’s not clear whether the word
“<span class="calibre28">`set`</span>” is a verb or an adjective.

The author intended <span class="calibre28">`set`</span> to be a verb,
but in the context of the <span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statement
it *feels* like an adjective. So the statement reads as “If the
<span class="calibre28">`username`</span> attribute was previously set
to <span class="calibre28">`unclebob`</span>” and not “set the
<span class="calibre28">`username`</span> attribute to
<span class="calibre28">`unclebob`</span> and if that worked then.…” We
<span id="Clean_Code_split_047.html_filepos245187"
class="calibre2"></span>could try to resolve this by renaming the
<span class="calibre28">`set`</span> function to
<span class="calibre28">`setAndCheckIfExists`</span>, but that doesn’t
much help the readability of the <span class="calibre28">`if`</span>
statement. The real solution is to separate the command from the query
so that the ambiguity cannot occur.

<span class="calibre28">   if (attributeExists(”username”)) {  
     setAttribute(”username”, ”unclebob”);  
     …  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_047.html_filepos245905"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_047.html_calibre_pb_58" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_048.html"></span>

### **Prefer Exceptions to Returning Error Codes**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Returning error codes from command functions is a subtle violation of
command query separation. It promotes commands being used as expressions
in the predicates of <span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statements.

<span class="calibre28">   if (deletePage(page) == E_OK)</span>

This does not suffer from verb/adjective confusion but does lead to
deeply nested structures. When you return an error code, you create the
problem that the caller must deal with the error immediately.

<span class="calibre28">   if (deletePage(page) == E_OK) {  
     if (registry.deleteReference(page.name) == E_OK) {  
       if (configKeys.deleteKey(page.name.makeKey()) == E_OK){  
         logger.log("page deleted");  
       } else {
