So I fired up Clover to see what the unit tests covered and what they
didn’t. Clover reported that the unit tests executed only 91 of the 185
executable statements in <span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span>
(~50 percent) \[T2\]. The coverage map looks like a patchwork quilt,
with big gobs of unexecuted code littered all through the class.

It was my goal to completely understand and also refactor this class. I
couldn’t do that without much greater test coverage. So I wrote my own
suite of completely independent unit tests
(<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1291372"
class="calibre18">Listing B-4</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1291451"
class="calibre18">374</a>).

As you look through these tests, you will note that many of them are
commented out. These tests didn’t pass. They represent behavior that I
think <span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span> should have. So as I
refactor <span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span>, I’ll be working to
make these tests pass too.

Even with some of the tests commented out, Clover reports that the new
unit tests are executing 170 (92 percent) out of the 185 executable
statements. This is pretty good, and I think we’ll be able to get this
number higher.

The first few commented-out tests (lines 23-63) were a bit of conceit on
my part. The program was not designed to pass these tests, but the
behavior seemed obvious \[G2\] to me.
<span id="Clean_Code_split_150.html_filepos998062"
class="calibre2"></span>I’m not sure why the
<span class="calibre28">`testWeekdayCodeToString`</span> method was
written in the first place, but because it is there, it seems obvious
that it should not be case sensitive. Writing these tests was trivial
\[T3\]. Making them pass was even easier; I just changed lines 259 and
263 to use <span class="calibre28">`equalsIgnoreCase`</span>.

I left the tests at line 32 and line 45 commented out because it’s not
clear to me that the “tues” and “thurs” abbreviations ought to be
supported.

The tests on line 153 and line 154 don’t pass. Clearly, they should
\[G2\]. We can easily fix this, and the tests on line 163 through line
213, by making the following changes to the
<span class="calibre28">`stringToMonthCode`</span> function.

<span class="calibre28">   457     if ((result \< 1) \|\| (result \>
12)) {  
               result = -1;  
   458         for (int i = 0; i \< monthNames.length; i++) {  
   459             if (s.equalsIgnoreCase(shortMonthNames\[i\])) {  
   460                 result = i + 1;  
   461                 break;  
   462             }  
   463             if (s.equalsIgnoreCase(monthNames\[i\])) {  
   464                 result = i + 1;  
   465                 break;  
   466             }  
   467         }  
   468     }</span>

The commented test on line 318 exposes a bug in the
<span class="calibre28">`getFollowingDayOfWeek`</span> method (line
672). December 25th, 2004, was a Saturday. The following Saturday was
January 1st, 2005. However, when we run the test, we see that
<span class="calibre28">`getFollowingDayOfWeek`</span> returns December
25th as the Saturday that follows December 25th. Clearly, this is wrong
\[G3\],\[T1\]. We see the problem in line 685. It is a typical boundary
condition error \[T5\]. It should read as follows:

<span class="calibre28">   685     if (baseDOW \>= targetWeekday)
{</span>

It is interesting to note that this function was the target of an
earlier repair. The change history (line 43) shows that “bugs” were
fixed in
<span class="calibre28">`getPreviousDayOfWeek, getFollowingDayOfWeek`</span>,
and <span class="calibre28">`getNearestDayOfWeek`</span> \[T6\].

The <span class="calibre28">`testGetNearestDayOfWeek`</span> unit test
(line 329), which tests the
<span class="calibre28">`getNearestDayOfWeek`</span> method (line 705),
did not start out as long and exhaustive as it currently is. I added a
lot of test cases to it because my initial test cases did not all pass
\[T6\]. You can see the pattern of failure by looking at which test
cases are commented out. That pattern is revealing \[T7\]. It shows that
the algorithm fails if the nearest day is in the future. Clearly there
is some kind of boundary condition error \[T5\].

The pattern of test coverage reported by Clover is also interesting
\[T8\]. Line 719 never gets executed! This means that the
<span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statement in line 718 is always
false. Sure enough, a look at the code shows that this must be true. The
<span class="calibre28">`adjust`</span> variable is always negative and
so cannot be greater or equal to 4. So this algorithm is just wrong.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_150.html_filepos1002824"
class="calibre2"></span>The right algorithm is shown below:

<span class="calibre28">     int delta = targetDOW -
base.getDayOfWeek();  
     int positiveDelta = delta + 7;  
     int adjust = positiveDelta % 7;  
     if (adjust \> 3)  
       adjust -= 7;  
  
     return SerialDate.addDays(adjust, base);</span>

Finally, the tests at line 417 and line 429 can be made to pass simply
by throwing an <span class="calibre28">`IllegalArgumentException`</span>
instead of returning an error string from
<span class="calibre28">`weekInMonthToString`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`relativeToString`</span>.

With these changes all the unit tests pass, and I believe
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span> now works. So now it’s time
to make it “right.”

<span id="Clean_Code_split_150.html_filepos1003982"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_150.html_calibre_pb_187" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html"></span>

### **Then Make It Right**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

We are going to walk from the top to the bottom of
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span>, improving it as we go
along. Although you won’t see this in the discussion, I will be running
all of the <span class="calibre28">`JCommon`</span> unit tests,
including my improved unit test for
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span>, after every change I make.
So rest assured that every change you see here works for all of
<span class="calibre28">`JCommon`</span>.

Starting at line 1, we see a ream of comments with license information,
copyrights, authors, and change history. I acknowledge that there are
certain legalities that need to be addressed, and so the copyrights and
licenses must stay. On the other hand, the change history is a leftover
from the 1960s. We have source code control tools that do this for us
now. This history should be deleted \[C1\].

The import list starting at line 61 could be shortened by using
<span class="calibre28">`java.text.*`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`java.util.*`</span>. \[J1\]

I wince at the HTML formatting in the Javadoc (line 67). Having a source
file with more than one language in it troubles me. This comment has
*four* languages in it: Java, English, Javadoc, and html \[G1\]. With
that many languages in use, it’s hard to keep things straight. For
example, the nice positioning of line 71 and line 72 are lost when the
Javadoc is generated, and yet who wants to see
<span class="calibre28">`<ul>`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`<li>`</span> in the source code? A better
strategy might be to just surround the whole comment with
<span class="calibre28">`<pre>`</span> so that the formatting that is
apparent in the source code is preserved within the Javadoc.<sup>1</sup>

<span class="calibre34">1. An even better solution would have been for
Javadoc to present all comments as preformatted, so that comments appear
the same in both code and document.</span>

Line 86 is the class declaration. Why is this class named
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span>? What is the significance of
the world “serial”? Is it because the class is derived from
<span class="calibre28">`Serializable`</span>? That doesn’t seem likely.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1006734"
class="calibre2"></span>I won’t keep you guessing. I know why (or at
least I think I know why) the word “serial” was used. The clue is in the
constants SERIAL_LOWER_BOUND and SERIAL_UPPER_BOUND on line 98 and line
101. An even better clue is in the comment that begins on line 830. This
class is named <span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span> because it is
implemented using a “serial number,” which happens to be the number of
days since December 30th, 1899.

I have two problems with this. First, the term “serial number” is not
really correct. This may be a quibble, but the representation is more of
a relative offset than a serial number. The term “serial number” has
more to do with product identification markers than dates. So I don’t
find this name particularly descriptive \[N1\]. A more descriptive term
might be “ordinal.”

The second problem is more significant. The name
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span> implies an implementation.
This class is an abstract class. There is no need to imply anything at
all about the implementation. Indeed, there is good reason to hide the
implementation! So I find this name to be at the wrong level of
abstraction \[N2\]. In my opinion, the name of this class should simply
be <span class="calibre28">`Date`</span>.

Unfortunately, there are already too many classes in the Java library
named <span class="calibre28">`Date`</span>, so this is probably not the
best name to choose. Because this class is all about days, instead of
time, I considered naming it <span class="calibre28">`Day`</span>, but
this name is also heavily used in other places. In the end, I chose
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> as the best compromise.

From now on in this discussion I will use the term
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. I leave it to you to remember
that the listings you are looking at still use
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span>.

I understand why <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> inherits from
<span class="calibre28">`Comparable`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`Serializable`</span>. But why does it inherit
from <span class="calibre28">`MonthConstants`</span>? The class
<span class="calibre28">`MonthConstants`</span>
(<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1290795"
class="calibre18">Listing B-3</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1290874"
class="calibre18">372</a>) is just a bunch of static final constants
that define the months. Inheriting from classes with constants is an old
trick that Java programmers used so that they could avoid using
expressions like
<span class="calibre28">`MonthConstants.January`</span>, but it’s a bad
idea \[J2\]. <span class="calibre28">`MonthConstants`</span> should
really be an enum.

<span class="calibre28">   public abstract class DayDate implements
Comparable,  
                                            Serializable {  
     public static enum Month {  
       JANUARY(1),  
       FEBRUARY(2),  
       MARCH(3),  
       APRIL(4),  
       MAY(5),  
       JUNE(6),  
       JULY(7),  
       AUGUST(8),  
       SEPTEMBER(9),  
       OCTOBER(10),  
       NOVEMBER(11),  
       DECEMBER(12);  
  
       Month(int index) {  
         this.index = index;  
       }  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1011120"
class="calibre2"></span>  
       public static Month make(int monthIndex) {  
         for (Month m : Month.values()) {  
           if (m.index == monthIndex)  
             return m;  
         }  
         throw new IllegalArgumentException(“Invalid month index ” +
monthIndex);  
       }  
       public final int index;  
     }</span>

Changing <span class="calibre28">`MonthConstants`</span> to this
<span class="calibre28">`enum`</span> forces quite a few changes to the
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> class and all it’s users. It
took me an hour to make all the changes. However, any function that used
to take an <span class="calibre28">`int`</span> for a month, now takes a
<span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enumerator. This means we can get
rid of the <span class="calibre28">`isValidMonthCode`</span> method
(line 326), and all the month code error checking such as that in
<span class="calibre28">`monthCodeToQuarter`</span> (line 356) \[G5\].

Next, we have line 91,
<span class="calibre28">`serialVersionUID`</span>. This variable is used
to control the serializer. If we change it, then any
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> written with an older version
of the software won’t be readable anymore and will result in an
<span class="calibre28">`InvalidClassException`</span>. If you don’t
declare the <span class="calibre28">`serialVersionUID`</span> variable,
then the compiler automatically generates one for you, and it will be
different every time you make a change to the module. I know that all
the documents recommend manual control of this variable, but it seems to
me that automatic control of serialization is a lot safer \[G4\]. After
all, I’d much rather debug an
<span class="calibre28">`InvalidClassException`</span> than the odd
behavior that would ensue if I forgot to change the
<span class="calibre28">`serialVersionUID`</span>. So I’m going to
delete the variable—at least for the time being.<sup>2</sup>

<span class="calibre34">2. Several of the reviewers of this text have
taken exception to this decision. They contend that in an open source
framework it is better to assert manual control over the serial ID so
that minor changes to the software don’t cause old serialized dates to
be invalid. This is a fair point. However, at least the failure,
inconvenient though it might be, has a clear-cut cause. On the other
hand, if the author of the class forgets to update the ID, then the
failure mode is undefined and might very well be silent. I think the
real moral of this story is that you should not expect to deserialize
across versions.</span>

I find the comment on line 93 redundant. Redundant comments are just
places to collect lies and misinformation \[C2\]. So I’m going to get
rid of it and its ilk.

The comments at line 97 and line 100 talk about serial numbers, which I
discussed earlier \[C1\]. The variables they describe are the earliest
and latest possible dates that <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>
can describe. This can be made a bit clearer \[N1\].

<span class="calibre28">   public static final int EARLIEST_DATE_ORDINAL
= 2;     // 1/1/1900  
   public static final int LATEST_DATE_ORDINAL = 2958465; //
12/31/9999</span>

It’s not clear to me why
<span class="calibre28">`EARLIEST_DATE_ORDINAL`</span> is 2 instead of
0. There is a hint in the comment on line 829 that suggests that this
has something to do with the way dates are represented in Microsoft
Excel. There is a much deeper insight provided in a derivative of
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> called
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>
(<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1292829"
class="calibre18">Listing B-5</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1292908"
class="calibre18">382</a>). The comment on line 71 describes the issue
nicely.

The problem I have with this is that the issue seems to be related to
the implementation of <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>
and has nothing to do with <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. I
conclude from this that
<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1016063"
class="calibre2"></span><span class="calibre28">`EARLIEST_DATE_ORDINAL`</span>
and <span class="calibre28">`LATEST_DATE_ORDINAL`</span> do not really
belong in <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> and should be moved
to <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span> \[G6\].

Indeed, a search of the code shows that these variables are used only
within <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>. Nothing in
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>, nor in any other class in the
<span class="calibre28">`JCommon`</span> framework, uses them.
Therefore, I’ll move them down into
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>.

The next variables,
<span class="calibre28">`MINIMUM_YEAR_SUPPORTED`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`MAXIMUM_YEAR_SUPPORTED`</span> (line 104 and
line 107), provide something of a dilemma. It seems clear that if
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> is an abstract class that
provides no foreshadowing of implementation, then it should not inform
us about a minimum or maximum year. Again, I am tempted to move these
variables down into <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>
\[G6\]. However, a quick search of the users of these variables shows
that one other class uses them:
<span class="calibre28">`RelativeDayOfWeekRule`</span>
(<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1294297"
class="calibre18">Listing B-6</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1294376"
class="calibre18">390</a>). We see that usage at line 177 and line 178
in the <span class="calibre28">`getDate`</span> function, where they are
used to check that the argument to
<span class="calibre28">`getDate`</span> is a valid year. The dilemma is
that a user of an abstract class needs information about its
implementation.

What we need to do is provide this information without polluting
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> itself. Usually, we would get
implementation information from an instance of a derivative. However,
the <span class="calibre28">`getDate`</span> function is not passed an
instance of a <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. It does,
however, return such an instance, which means that somewhere it must be
creating it. Line 187 through line 205 provide the hint. The
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> instance is being created by
one of the three functions,
<span class="calibre28">`getPreviousDayOfWeek, getNearestDayOfWeek`</span>,
or <span class="calibre28">`getFollowingDayOfWeek`</span>. Looking back
at the <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> listing, we see that
these functions (lines 638–724) all return a date created by
<span class="calibre28">`addDays`</span> (line 571), which calls
<span class="calibre28">`createInstance`</span> (line 808), which
creates a <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>! \[G7\].

It’s generally a bad idea for base classes to know about their
derivatives. To fix this, we should use the
A<span class="small">BSTRACT</span>
F<span class="small">ACTORY</span><sup>3</sup> pattern and create a
<span class="calibre28">`DayDateFactory`</span>. This factory will
create the instances of <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> that we
need and can also answer questions about the implementation, such as the
maximum and minimum dates.

<span class="calibre34">3.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1019559"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_153.html_filepos1064379"
class="calibre18">GOF</a>\].</span>

<span class="calibre28">   public abstract class DayDateFactory {  
     private static DayDateFactory factory = new
SpreadsheetDateFactory();  
     public static void setInstance(DayDateFactory factory) {  
       DayDateFactory.factory = factory;  
     }  
  
     protected abstract DayDate \_makeDate(int ordinal);  
     protected abstract DayDate \_makeDate(int day, DayDate.Month month,
int year);  
     protected abstract DayDate \_makeDate(int day, int month, int
year);  
     protected abstract DayDate \_makeDate(java.util.Date date);  
     protected abstract int \_getMinimumYear();  
     protected abstract int \_getMaximumYear();  
  
     public static DayDate makeDate(int ordinal) {  
       return factory.\_makeDate(ordinal);  
     }<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1020797"
class="calibre2"></span>  
     public static DayDate makeDate(int day, DayDate.Month month, int
year) {  
       return factory.\_makeDate(day, month, year);  
     }  
  
     public static DayDate makeDate(int day, int month, int year) {  
       return factory.\_makeDate(day, month, year);  
     }  
  
     public static DayDate makeDate(java.util.Date date) {  
       return factory.\_makeDate(date);  
     }  
  
     public static int getMinimumYear() {  
       return factory.\_getMinimumYear();  
     }  
  
     public static int getMaximumYear() {  
       return factory.\_getMaximumYear();  
     }  
   }</span>

This factory class replaces the
<span class="calibre28">`createInstance`</span> methods with
<span class="calibre28">`makeDate`</span> methods, which improves the
names quite a bit \[N1\]. It defaults to a
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDateFactory`</span> but can be
changed at any time to use a different factory. The static methods that
delegate to abstract methods use a combination of the
S<span class="small">INGLETON</span>,<sup>4</sup>
D<span class="small">ECORATOR</span>,<sup>5</sup> and
A<span class="small">BSTRACT</span> F<span class="small">ACTORY</span>
patterns that I have found to be useful.

<span class="calibre34">4. Ibid.</span>

<span class="calibre34">5. Ibid.</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDateFactory`</span> looks like
this.

<span class="calibre28">   public class SpreadsheetDateFactory extends
DayDateFactory {  
     public DayDate \_makeDate(int ordinal) {  
       return new SpreadsheetDate(ordinal);  
     }  
  
     public DayDate \_makeDate(int day, DayDate.Month month, int year)
{  
       return new SpreadsheetDate(day, month, year);  
     }  
  
     public DayDate \_makeDate(int day, int month, int year) {  
       return new SpreadsheetDate(day, month, year);  
     }  
  
     public DayDate \_makeDate(Date date) {  
       final GregorianCalendar calendar = new GregorianCalendar();  
       calendar.setTime(date);  
       return new SpreadsheetDate(  
         calendar.get(Calendar.DATE),  
         DayDate.Month.make(calendar.get(Calendar.MONTH) + 1),  
         calendar.get(Calendar.YEAR));  
     }  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1024286"
class="calibre2"></span>  
     protected int \_getMinimumYear() {  
       return SpreadsheetDate.MINIMUM_YEAR_SUPPORTED;  
     }  
  
     protected int \_getMaximumYear() {  
       return SpreadsheetDate.MAXIMUM_YEAR_SUPPORTED;  
     }  
   }</span>

As you can see, I have already moved the
<span class="calibre28">`MINIMUM_YEAR_SUPPORTED`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`MAXIMUM_YEAR_SUPPORTED`</span> variables into
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>, where they belong
\[G6\].

The next issue in <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> are the day
constants beginning at line 109. These should really be another enum
\[J3\]. We’ve seen this pattern before, so I won’t repeat it here.
You’ll see it in the final listings.

Next, we see a series of tables starting with
<span class="calibre28">`LAST_DAY_OF_MONTH`</span> at line 140. My first
issue with these tables is that the comments that describe them are
redundant \[C3\]. Their names are sufficient. So I’m going to delete the
comments.

There seems to be no good reason that this table isn’t private \[G8\],
because there is a static function
<span class="calibre28">`lastDayOfMonth`</span> that provides the same
data.

The next table,
<span class="calibre28">`AGGREGATE_DAYS_TO_END_OF_MONTH`</span>, is a
bit more mysterious because it is not used anywhere in the
<span class="calibre28">`JCommon`</span> framework \[G9\]. So I deleted
it.

The same goes for
<span class="calibre28">`LEAP_YEAR_AGGREGATE_DAYS_TO_END_OF_MONTH`</span>.

The next table,
<span class="calibre28">`AGGREGATE_DAYS_TO_END_OF_PRECEDING_MONTH`</span>,
is used only in <span class="calibre28">`Spread-sheetDate`</span> (line
434 and line 473). This begs the question of whether it should be moved
to <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>. The argument for
not moving it is that the table is not specific to any particular
implementation \[G6\]. On the other hand, no implementation other than
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span> actually exists, and so
the table should be moved close to where it is used \[G10\].

What settles the argument for me is that to be consistent \[G11\], we
should make the table private and expose it through a function like
<span class="calibre28">`julianDateOfLastDayOfMonth`</span>. Nobody
seems to need a function like that. Moreover, the table can be moved
back to <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> easily if any new
implementation of <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> needs it. So
I moved it.

The same goes for the table,
<span class="calibre28">`LEAP_YEAR_AGGREGATE_DAYS_TO_END_OF_MONTH`</span>.

Next, we see three sets of constants that can be turned into enums
(lines 162–205). The first of the three selects a week within a month. I
changed it into an enum named
<span class="calibre28">`WeekInMonth`</span>.

<span class="calibre28">   public enum WeekInMonth {  
       FIRST(1), SECOND(2), THIRD(3), FOURTH(4), LAST(0);  
       public final int index;  
  
       WeekInMonth(int index) {  
         this.index = index;  
       }  
     }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1028506"
class="calibre2"></span>The second set of constants (lines 177–187) is a
bit more obscure. The
<span class="calibre28">`INCLUDE_NONE, INCLUDE_FIRST, INCLUDE_SECOND`</span>,
and <span class="calibre28">`INCLUDE_BOTH`</span> constants are used to
describe whether the defining end-point dates of a range should be
included in that range. Mathematically, this is described using the
terms “open interval,” “half-open interval,” and “closed interval.” I
think it is clearer using the mathematical nomenclature \[N3\], so I
changed it to an enum named
<span class="calibre28">`DateInterval`</span> with
<span class="calibre28">`CLOSED, CLOSED_LEFT, CLOSED_RIGHT`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`OPEN`</span> enumerators.

The third set of constants (lines 18–205) describe whether a search for
a particular day of the week should result in the last, next, or nearest
instance. Deciding what to call this is difficult at best. In the end, I
settled for <span class="calibre28">`WeekdayRange`</span> with
<span class="calibre28">`LAST, NEXT`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`NEAREST`</span> enumerators.

You might not agree with the names I’ve chosen. They make sense to me,
but they may not make sense to you. The point is that they are now in a
form that makes them easy to change \[J3\]. They aren’t passed as
integers anymore; they are passed as symbols. I can use the “change
name” function of my IDE to change the names, or the types, without
worrying that I missed some <span class="calibre28">`-1`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`2`</span> somewhere in the code or that some
<span class="calibre28">`int`</span> argument declaration is left poorly
described.

The description field at line 208 does not seem to be used by anyone. I
deleted it along with its accessor and mutator \[G9\].

I also deleted the degenerate default constructor at line 213 \[G12\].
The compiler will generate it for us.

We can skip over the <span class="calibre28">`isValidWeekdayCode`</span>
method (lines 216–238) because we deleted it when we created the
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> enumeration.

This brings us to the
<span class="calibre28">`stringToWeekdayCode`</span> method (lines
242–270). Javadocs that don’t add much to the method signature are just
clutter \[C3\],\[G12\]. The only value this Javadoc adds is the
description of the <span class="calibre28">`-1`</span> return value.
However, because we changed to the <span class="calibre28">`Day`</span>
enumeration, the comment is actually wrong \[C2\]. The method now throws
an <span class="calibre28">`IllegalArgumentException`</span>. So I
deleted the Javadoc.

I also deleted all the <span class="calibre28">`final`</span> keywords
in arguments and variable declarations. As far as I could tell, they
added no real value but did add to the clutter \[G12\]. Eliminating
<span class="calibre28">`final`</span> flies in the face of some
conventional wisdom. For example, Robert Simmons<sup>6</sup> strongly
recommends us to “… spread <span class="calibre28">`final`</span> all
over your code.” Clearly I disagree. I think that there are a few good
uses for <span class="calibre28">`final`</span>, such as the occasional
<span class="calibre28">`final`</span> constant, but otherwise the
keyword adds little value and creates a lot of clutter. Perhaps I feel
this way because the kinds of errors that
<span class="calibre28">`final`</span> might catch are already caught by
the unit tests I write.

<span class="calibre34">6.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1032549"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_153.html_filepos1064659"
class="calibre18">Simmons04</a>\], p.
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_059.html_filepos339554"
class="calibre18">73</a>.</span>

I didn’t care for the duplicate <span class="calibre28">`if`</span>
statements \[G5\] inside the <span class="calibre28">`for`</span> loop
(line 259 and line 263), so I connected them into a single
<span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statement using the
<span class="calibre28">`||`</span> operator. I also used the
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> enumeration to direct the
<span class="calibre28">`for`</span> loop and made a few other cosmetic
changes.

It occurred to me that this method does not really belong in
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. It’s really the parse function
of <span class="calibre28">`Day`</span>. So I moved it into the
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> enumeration. However, that made the
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span>
<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1033546"
class="calibre2"></span>enumeration pretty large. Because the concept of
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> does not depend on
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>, I moved the
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> enumeration outside of the
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> class into its own source file
\[G13\].

I also moved the next function,
<span class="calibre28">`weekdayCodeToString`</span> (lines 272–286)
into the <span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> enumeration and called it
<span class="calibre28">`toString`</span>.

<span class="calibre28">   public enum Day {  
     MONDAY(Calendar.MONDAY),  
     TUESDAY(Calendar.TUESDAY),  
     WEDNESDAY(Calendar.WEDNESDAY),s  
     THURSDAY(Calendar.THURSDAY),  
     FRIDAY(Calendar.FRIDAY),  
     SATURDAY(Calendar.SATURDAY),  
     SUNDAY(Calendar.SUNDAY);  
  
     public final int index;  
     private static DateFormatSymbols dateSymbols = new
DateFormatSymbols();  
  
     Day(int day) {  
       index = day;  
     }  
  
     public static Day make(int index) throws IllegalArgumentException
{  
       for (Day d : Day.values())  
         if (d.index == index)  
           return d;  
       throw new IllegalArgumentException(  
         String.format(“Illegal day index: %d.”, index));  
     }  
  
     public static Day parse(String s) throws IllegalArgumentException
{  
       String\[\] shortWeekdayNames =  
         dateSymbols.getShortWeekdays();  
       String\[\] weekDayNames =  
         dateSymbols.getWeekdays();  
  
       s = s.trim();  
       for (Day day : Day.values()) {  
         if (s.equalsIgnoreCase(shortWeekdayNames\[day.index\]) \|\|  
             s.equalsIgnoreCase(weekDayNames\[day.index\])) {  
           return day;  
         }  
       }  
       throw new IllegalArgumentException(  
         String.format(“%s is not a valid weekday string”, s));  
      }  
  
      public String toString() {  
        return dateSymbols.getWeekdays()\[index\];  
      }  
    }</span>

There are two <span class="calibre28">`getMonths`</span> functions
(lines 288–316). The first calls the second. The second is never called
by anyone but the first. Therefore, I collapsed the two into one and
vastly simplified them \[G9\],\[G12\],\[F4\]. Finally, I changed the
name to be a bit more self-descriptive \[N1\].

<span class="calibre28"><span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1037531"
class="calibre2"></span>   public static String\[\] getMonthNames() {  
     return dateFormatSymbols.getMonths();  
   }</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`isValidMonthCode`</span> function (lines
326–346) was made irrelevant by the
<span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enum, so I deleted it \[G9\].

The <span class="calibre28">`monthCodeToQuarter`</span> function (lines
356–375) smells of F<span class="small">EATURE</span>
E<span class="small">NVY</span><sup>7</sup> \[G14\] and probably belongs
in the <span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enum as a method named
<span class="calibre28">`quarter`</span>. So I replaced it.

<span class="calibre34">7.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1038384"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_153.html_filepos1064903"
class="calibre18">Refactoring</a>\].</span>

<span class="calibre28">   public int quarter() {  
     return 1 + (index-1)/3;  
   }</span>

This made the <span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enum big enough to
be in its own class. So I moved it out of
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> to be consistent with the
<span class="calibre28">`Day`</span> enum \[G11\],\[G13\].

The next two methods are named
<span class="calibre28">`monthCodeToString`</span> (lines 377–426).
Again, we see the pattern of one method calling its twin with a flag. It
is usually a bad idea to pass a flag as an argument to a function,
especially when that flag simply selects the format of the output
\[G15\]. I renamed, simplified, and restructured these functions and
moved them into the <span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enum
\[N1\],\[N3\],\[C3\],\[G14\].

<span class="calibre28">   public String toString() {  
     return dateFormatSymbols.getMonths()\[index - 1\];  
   }  
  
   public String toShortString() {  
     return dateFormatSymbols.getShortMonths()\[index - 1\];  
   }</span>

The next method is <span class="calibre28">`stringToMonthCode`</span>
(lines 428–472). I renamed it, moved it into the
<span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enum, and simplified it
\[N1\],\[N3\],\[C3\],\[G14\],\[G12\].

<span class="calibre28">   public static Month parse(String s) {  
     s = s.trim();  
     for (Month m : Month.values())  
       if (m.matches(s))  
         return m;  
  
     try {  
       return make(Integer.parseInt(s));  
     }  
     catch (NumberFormatException e) {}  
     throw new IllegalArgumentException(“Invalid month ” + s);  
   }  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1040875"
class="calibre2"></span>  
   private boolean matches(String s) {  
     return s.equalsIgnoreCase(toString()) \|\|  
            s.equalsIgnoreCase(toShortString());  
   }</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`isLeapYear`</span> method (lines 495–517)
can be made a bit more expressive \[G16\].

<span class="calibre28">   public static boolean isLeapYear(int year)
{  
     boolean fourth = year % 4 == 0;  
     boolean hundredth = year % 100 == 0;  
     boolean fourHundredth = year % 400 == 0;  
     return fourth && (!hundredth \|\| fourHundredth);  
   }</span>

The next function, <span class="calibre28">`leapYearCount`</span> (lines
519–536) doesn’t really belong in
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. Nobody calls it except for two
methods in <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>. So I pushed
it down \[G6\].

The <span class="calibre28">`lastDayOfMonth`</span> function (lines
538–560) makes use of the
<span class="calibre28">`LAST_DAY_OF_MONTH`</span> array. This array
really belongs in the <span class="calibre28">`Month`</span> enum
\[G17\], so I moved it there. I also simplified the function and made it
a bit more expressive \[G16\].

<span class="calibre28">   public static int lastDayOfMonth(Month month,
int year) {  
     if (month == Month.FEBRUARY && isLeapYear(year))  
       return month.lastDay() + 1;  
      else  
       return month.lastDay();  
   }</span>

Now things start to get a bit more interesting. The next function is
<span class="calibre28">`addDays`</span> (lines 562–576). First of all,
because this function operates on the variables of
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>, it should not be static
\[G18\]. So I changed it to an instance method. Second, it calls the
function <span class="calibre28">`toSerial`</span>. This function should
be renamed <span class="calibre28">`toOrdinal`</span> \[N1\]. Finally,
the method can be simplified.

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate addDays(int days) {  
     return DayDateFactory.makeDate(toOrdinal() + days);  
   }</span>

The same goes for <span class="calibre28">`addMonths`</span> (lines
578–602). It should be an instance method \[G18\]. The algorithm is a
bit complicated, so I used E<span class="small">XPLAINING</span>
T<span class="small">EMPORARY</span>
V<span class="small">ARIABLES</span><sup>8</sup> \[G19\] to make it more
transparent. I also renamed the method
<span class="calibre28">`getYYY`</span> to
<span class="calibre28">`getYear`</span> \[N1\].

<span class="calibre34">8.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1044352"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_153.html_filepos1065187"
class="calibre18">Beck97</a>\].</span>

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate addMonths(int months) {  
     int thisMonthAsOrdinal = 12 \* getYear() + getMonth().index - 1;  
     int resultMonthAsOrdinal = thisMonthAsOrdinal + months;  
     int resultYear = resultMonthAsOrdinal / 12;  
     Month resultMonth = Month.make(resultMonthAsOrdinal % 12 + 1);  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1044897"
class="calibre2"></span>  
     int lastDayOfResultMonth = lastDayOfMonth(resultMonth,
resultYear);  
     int resultDay = Math.min(getDayOfMonth(), lastDayOfResultMonth);  
     return DayDateFactory.makeDate(resultDay, resultMonth,
resultYear);  
   }</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`addYears`</span> function (lines 604–626)
provides no surprises over the others.

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate plusYears(int years) {  
     int resultYear = getYear() + years;  
     int lastDayOfMonthInResultYear = lastDayOfMonth(getMonth(),
resultYear);  
     int resultDay = Math.min(getDayOfMonth(),
lastDayOfMonthInResultYear);  
     return DayDateFactory.makeDate(resultDay, getMonth(),
resultYear);  
   }</span>

There is a little itch at the back of my mind that is bothering me about
changing these methods from static to instance. Does the expression
<span class="calibre28">`date.addDays(5)`</span> make it clear that the
<span class="calibre28">`date`</span> object does not change and that a
new instance of <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> is returned? Or
does it erroneously imply that we are adding five days to the
<span class="calibre28">`date`</span> object? You might not think that
is a big problem, but a bit of code that looks like the following can be
very deceiving \[G20\].

<span class="calibre28">   DayDate date = DateFactory.makeDate(5,
Month.DECEMBER, 1952);  
   date.addDays(7); // bump date by one week.</span>

Someone reading this code would very likely just accept that
<span class="calibre28">`addDays`</span> is changing the
<span class="calibre28">`date`</span> object. So we need a name that
breaks this ambiguity \[N4\]. So I changed the names to
<span class="calibre28">`plusDays`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`plusMonths`</span>. It seems to me that the
intent of the method is captured nicely by

<span class="calibre28">   DayDate date = oldDate.plusDays(5);</span>

whereas the following doesn’t read fluidly enough for a reader to simply
accept that the <span class="calibre28">`date`</span> object is changed:

<span class="calibre28">   date.plusDays(5);</span>

The algorithms continue to get more interesting.
<span class="calibre28">`getPreviousDayOfWeek`</span> (lines 628–660)
works but is a bit complicated. After some thought about what was really
going on \[G21\], I was able to simplify it and use
E<span class="small">XPLAINING</span>
T<span class="small">EMPORARY</span>
V<span class="small">ARIABLES</span> \[G19\] to make it clearer. I also
changed it from a static method to an instance method \[G18\], and got
rid of the duplicate instance method \[G5\] (lines 997–1008).

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate getPreviousDayOfWeek(Day
targetDayOfWeek) {  
     int offsetToTarget = targetDayOfWeek.index -
getDayOfWeek().index;  
     if (offsetToTarget \>= 0)  
       offsetToTarget -= 7;  
     return plusDays(offsetToTarget);  
   }</span>

The exact same analysis and result occurred for
<span class="calibre28">`getFollowingDayOfWeek`</span> (lines 662–693).

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate getFollowingDayOfWeek(Day
targetDayOfWeek) {  
       int offsetToTarget = targetDayOfWeek.index -
getDayOfWeek().index;  
       if (offsetToTarget \<= 0)  
<span class="calibre2"></span>  
         offsetToTarget += 7;  
       return plusDays(offsetToTarget);  
     }</span>

The next function is
<span class="calibre28">`getNearestDayOfWeek`</span> (lines 695–726),
which we corrected back on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_150.html_filepos1002824"
class="calibre18">270</a>. But the changes I made back then aren’t
consistent with the current pattern in the last two functions \[G11\].
So I made it consistent and used some
E<span class="small">XPLAINING</span>
T<span class="small">EMPORARY</span>
V<span class="small">ARIABLES</span> \[G19\] to clarify the algorithm.

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate getNearestDayOfWeek(final Day
targetDay) {  
       int offsetToThisWeeksTarget = targetDay.index -
getDayOfWeek().index;  
       int offsetToFutureTarget = (offsetToThisWeeksTarget + 7) % 7;  
       int offsetToPreviousTarget = offsetToFutureTarget - 7;  
  
       if (offsetToFutureTarget \> 3)  
         return plusDays(offsetToPreviousTarget);  
       else  
         return plusDays(offsetToFutureTarget);  
     }</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`getEndOfCurrentMonth`</span> method (lines
728–740) is a little strange because it is an instance method that
envies \[G14\] its own class by taking a
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> argument. I made it a true
instance method and clarified a few names.

<span class="calibre28">   public DayDate getEndOfMonth() {  
       Month month = getMonth();  
       int year = getYear();  
       int lastDay = lastDayOfMonth(month, year);  
       return DayDateFactory.makeDate(lastDay, month, year);  
     }</span>

Refactoring <span class="calibre28">`weekInMonthToString`</span> (lines
742–761) turned out to be very interesting indeed. Using the refactoring
tools of my IDE, I first moved the method to the
<span class="calibre28">`WeekInMonth`</span> enum that I created back on
page <a href="#Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1024286"
class="calibre18">275</a>. Then I renamed the method to
<span class="calibre28">`toString`</span>. Next, I changed it from a
static method to an instance method. All the tests still passed. (Can
you guess where I am going?)

Next, I deleted the method entirely! Five asserts failed (lines 411–415,
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1291372"
class="calibre18">Listing B-4</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1291451"
class="calibre18">374</a>). I changed these lines to use the names of
the enumerators (<span class="calibre28">`FIRST, SECOND`</span>, …). All
the tests passed. Can you see why? Can you also see why each of these
steps was necessary? The refactoring tool made sure that all previous
callers of <span class="calibre28">`weekInMonthToString`</span> now
called <span class="calibre28">`toString`</span> on the
<span class="calibre28">`weekInMonth`</span> enumerator because all
enumerators implement <span class="calibre28">`toString`</span> to
simply return their names.…

Unfortunately, I was a bit too clever. As elegant as that wonderful
chain of refactorings was, I finally realized that the only users of
this function were the tests I had just modified, so I deleted the
tests.

Fool me once, shame on you. Fool me twice, shame on me! So after
determining that nobody other than the tests called
<span class="calibre28">`relativeToString`</span> (lines 765–781), I
simply deleted the function and its tests.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1053643"
class="calibre2"></span>We have finally made it to the abstract methods
of this abstract class. And the first one is as appropriate as they
come: <span class="calibre28">`toSerial`</span> (lines 838–844). Back on
page <a href="#Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1040875"
class="calibre18">279</a> I had changed the name to
<span class="calibre28">`toOrdinal`</span>. Having looked at it in this
context, I decided the name should be changed to
<span class="calibre28">`getOrdinalDay`</span>.

The next abstract method is <span class="calibre28">`toDate`</span>
(lines 838–844). It converts a <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>
to a <span class="calibre28">`java.util.Date`</span>. Why is this method
abstract? If we look at its implementation in
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span> (lines 198–207,
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1292829"
class="calibre18">Listing B-5</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1292908"
class="calibre18">382</a>), we see that it doesn’t depend on anything in
the implementation of that class \[G6\]. So I pushed it up.

The <span class="calibre28">`getYYYY, getMonth`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`getDayOfMonth`</span> methods are nicely
abstract. However, the <span class="calibre28">`getDayOfWeek`</span>
method is another one that should be pulled up from
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadSheetDate`</span> because it doesn’t
depend on anything that can’t be found in
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> \[G6\]. Or does it?

If you look carefully (line 247,
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1292829"
class="calibre18">Listing B-5</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1292908"
class="calibre18">382</a>), you’ll see that the algorithm implicitly
depends on the origin of the ordinal day (in other words, the day of the
week of day 0). So even though this function has no physical
dependencies that couldn’t be moved to
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>, it does have a logical
dependency.

Logical dependencies like this bother me \[G22\]. If something logical
depends on the implementation, then something physical should too. Also,
it seems to me that the algorithm itself could be generic with a much
smaller portion of it dependent on the implementation \[G6\].

So I created an abstract method in
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> named
<span class="calibre28">`getDayOfWeekForOrdinalZero`</span> and
implemented it in <span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span> to
return <span class="calibre28">`Day.SATURDAY`</span>. Then I moved the
<span class="calibre28">`getDayOfWeek`</span> method up to
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> and changed it to call
<span class="calibre28">`getOrdinalDay`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`getDayOfWeekForOrdinal-Zero`</span>.

<span class="calibre28">   public Day getDayOfWeek() {  
       Day startingDay = getDayOfWeekForOrdinalZero();  
       int startingOffset = startingDay.index - Day.SUNDAY.index;  
       return Day.make((getOrdinalDay() + startingOffset) % 7 + 1);  
     }</span>

As a side note, look carefully at the comment on line 895 through line
899. Was this repetition really necessary? As usual, I deleted this
comment along with all the others.

The next method is <span class="calibre28">`compare`</span> (lines
902–913). Again, this method is inappropriately abstract \[G6\], so I
pulled the implementation up into
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. Also, the name does not
communicate enough \[N1\]. This method actually returns the difference
in days since the argument. So I changed the name to
<span class="calibre28">`daysSince`</span>. Also, I noted that there
weren’t any tests for this method, so I wrote them.

The next six functions (lines 915–980) are all abstract methods that
should be implemented in <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>. So I
pulled them all up from
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>.

The last function, <span class="calibre28">`isInRange`</span> (lines
982–995) also needs to be pulled up and refactored. The
<span class="calibre28">`switch`</span> statement is a bit ugly \[G23\]
and can be replaced by moving the cases into the
<span class="calibre28">`DateInterval`</span> enum.

<span class="calibre28"><span class="calibre2"></span>   public enum
DateInterval {  
       OPEN {  
         public boolean isIn(int d, int left, int right) {  
           return d \> left && d \< right;  
         }  
       },  
       CLOSED_LEFT {  
         public boolean isIn(int d, int left, int right) {  
           return d \>= left && d \< right;  
         }  
       },  
       CLOSED_RIGHT {  
         public boolean isIn(int d, int left, int right) {  
           return d \> left && d \<= right;  
         }  
       },  
       CLOSED {  
         public boolean isIn(int d, int left, int right) {  
           return d \>= left && d \<= right;  
         }  
       };  
  
       public abstract boolean isIn(int d, int left, int right);  
     }</span>

<span class="calibre28">   public boolean isInRange(DayDate d1, DayDate
d2, DateInterval interval) {  
       int left = Math.min(d1.getOrdinalDay(), d2.getOrdinalDay());  
       int right = Math.max(d1.getOrdinalDay(), d2.getOrdinalDay());  
       return interval.isIn(getOrdinalDay(), left, right);  
     }</span>

That brings us to the end of <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span>.
So now we’ll make one more pass over the whole class to see how well it
flows.

First, the opening comment is long out of date, so I shortened and
improved it \[C2\].

Next, I moved all the remaining enums out into their own files \[G12\].

Next, I moved the static variable
(<span class="calibre28">`dateFormatSymbols`</span>) and three static
methods
(<span class="calibre28">`getMonthNames, isLeapYear, lastDayOfMonth`</span>)
into a new class named <span class="calibre28">`DateUtil`</span> \[G6\].

I moved the abstract methods up to the top where they belong \[G24\].

I changed <span class="calibre28">`Month.make`</span> to
<span class="calibre28">`Month.fromInt`</span> \[N1\] and did the same
for all the other enums. I also created a
<span class="calibre28">`toInt()`</span> accessor for all the enums and
made the <span class="calibre28">`index`</span> field private.

There was some interesting duplication \[G5\] in
<span class="calibre28">`plusYears`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`plusMonths`</span> that I was able to eliminate
by extracting a new method named
<span class="calibre28">`correctLastDayOfMonth`</span>, making the all
three methods much clearer.

I got rid of the magic number 1 \[G25\], replacing it with
<span class="calibre28">`Month.JANUARY.toInt()`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`Day.SUNDAY.toInt()`</span>, as appropriate. I
spent a little time with
<span class="calibre28">`SpreadsheetDate`</span>, cleaning up the
algorithms a bit. The end result is contained in
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1295173"
class="calibre18">Listing B-7</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1295252"
class="calibre18">394</a>, through
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1299418"
class="calibre18">Listing B-16</a>, page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1299497"
class="calibre18">405</a>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1063065"
class="calibre2"></span>Interestingly the code coverage in
<span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> has *decreased* to 84.9
percent! This is not because less functionality is being tested; rather
it is because the class has shrunk so much that the few uncovered lines
have a greater weight. <span class="calibre28">`DayDate`</span> now has
45 out of 53 executable statements covered by tests. The uncovered lines
are so trivial that they weren’t worth testing.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_filepos1063582"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_151.html_calibre_pb_188" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_152.html"></span>

### **Conclusion**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

So once again we’ve followed the Boy Scout Rule. We’ve checked the code
in a bit cleaner than when we checked it out. It took a little time, but
it was worth it. Test coverage was increased, some bugs were fixed, the
code was clarified and shrunk. The next person to look at this code will
hopefully find it easier to deal with than we did. That person will also
probably be able to clean it up a bit more than we did.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_152.html_filepos1064223"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_152.html_calibre_pb_189" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_153.html"></span>

### **Bibliography**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_154.html"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_154.html_filepos1065453" style="height:0pt">

</div>

## <span id="Clean_Code_split_154.html_filepos1065522" class="calibre10"></span>**17 Smells and Heuristics**

<div class="calibre21">

 

</div>

<img src="media/images/00049.jpg" class="calibre4" alt="Image" />

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In his wonderful book *Refactoring*,<sup>1</sup> Martin Fowler
identified many different “Code Smells.” The list that follows includes
many of Martin’s smells and adds many more of my own. It also includes
other pearls and heuristics that I use to practice my trade.

<span class="calibre34">1.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_154.html_filepos1066124"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1175798"
class="calibre18">Refactoring</a>\].</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_154.html_filepos1066235"
class="calibre2"></span>I compiled this list by walking through several
different programs and refactoring them. As I made each change, I asked
myself *why* I made that change and then wrote the reason down here. The
result is a rather long list of things that smell bad to me when I read
code.

This list is meant to be read from top to bottom and also to be used as
a reference. There is a cross-reference for each heuristic that shows
you where it is referenced in the rest of the text in “Appendix C” on
page <a href="#Clean_Code_split_176.html_filepos1300237"
class="calibre18">409</a>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_154.html_filepos1066893"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_154.html_calibre_pb_193" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html"></span>

### **Comments**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1067045"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **C1: *Inappropriate Information***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It is inappropriate for a comment to hold information better held in a
different kind of system such as your source code control system, your
issue tracking system, or any other record-keeping system. Change
histories, for example, just clutter up source files with volumes of
historical and uninteresting text. In general, meta-data such as
authors, last-modified-date, SPR number, and so on should not appear in
comments. Comments should be reserved for technical notes about the code
and design.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1067766"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **C2: *Obsolete Comment***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

A comment that has gotten old, irrelevant, and incorrect is obsolete.
Comments get old quickly. It is best not to write a comment that will
become obsolete. If you find an obsolete comment, it is best to update
it or get rid of it as quickly as possible. Obsolete comments tend to
migrate away from the code they once described. They become floating
islands of irrelevance and misdirection in the code.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1068382"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **C3: *Redundant Comment***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

A comment is redundant if it describes something that adequately
describes itself. For example:

<span class="calibre28">   i++; // increment i</span>

Another example is a Javadoc that says nothing more than (or even less
than) the function signature:

<span class="calibre28">   /\*\*  
    \* @param sellRequest  
    \* @return  
    \* @throws ManagedComponentException  
    \*/  
   public SellResponse beginSellItem(SellRequest sellRequest)  
     throws ManagedComponentException</span>

Comments should say things that the code cannot say for itself.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1069515"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1069568" class="calibre2"></span>**C4: *Poorly Written Comment***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

A comment worth writing is worth writing well. If you are going to write
a comment, take the time to make sure it is the best comment you can
write. Choose your words carefully. Use correct grammar and punctuation.
Don’t ramble. Don’t state the obvious. Be brief.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1070017"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **C5: *Commented-Out Code***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It makes me crazy to see stretches of code that are commented out. Who
knows how old it is? Who knows whether or not it’s meaningful? Yet no
one will delete it because everyone assumes someone else needs it or has
plans for it.

That code sits there and rots, getting less and less relevant with every
passing day. It calls functions that no longer exist. It uses variables
whose names have changed. It follows conventions that are long obsolete.
It pollutes the modules that contain it and distracts the people who try
to read it. Commented-out code is an *abomination*.

When you see commented-out code, *delete it!* Don’t worry, the source
code control system still remembers it. If anyone really needs it, he or
she can go back and check out a previous version. Don’t suffer
commented-out code to survive.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_filepos1071184"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_155.html_calibre_pb_194" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_156.html"></span>

### **Environment**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_156.html_filepos1071339"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **E1: *Build Requires More Than One Step***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Building a project should be a single trivial operation. You should not
have to check many little pieces out from source code control. You
should not need a sequence of arcane commands or context dependent
scripts in order to build the individual elements. You should not have
to search near and far for all the various little extra JARs, XML files,
and other artifacts that the system requires. You *should* be able to
check out the system with one simple command and then issue one other
simple command to build it.

<span class="calibre28">   svn get mySystem  
   cd mySystem  
   ant all</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_156.html_filepos1072271"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **E2: *Tests Require More Than One Step***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

You should be able to run *all* the unit tests with just one command. In
the best case you can run all the tests by clicking on one button in
your IDE. In the worst case you should be able to issue a single simple
command in a shell. Being able to run all the tests is so fundamental
and so important that it should be quick, easy, and obvious to do.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_156.html_filepos1072858"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_156.html_calibre_pb_195" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html"></span>

### <span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_filepos1072927" class="calibre10"></span>**Functions**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_filepos1073018"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **F1: *Too Many Arguments***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Functions should have a small number of arguments. No argument is best,
followed by one, two, and three. More than three is very questionable
and should be avoided with prejudice. (See
“<a href="#Clean_Code_split_044.html_filepos222427"
class="calibre18">Function Arguments</a>” on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_044.html_filepos220877"
class="calibre18">40</a>.)

<span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_filepos1073518"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **F2: *Output Arguments***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Output arguments are counterintuitive. Readers expect arguments to be
inputs, not outputs. If your function must change the state of
something, have it change the state of the object it is called on. (See
“<a href="#Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos240794"
class="calibre18">Output Arguments</a>” on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_046.html_filepos240847"
class="calibre18">45</a>.)

<span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_filepos1074034"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **F3: *Flag Arguments***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Boolean arguments loudly declare that the function does more than one
thing. They are confusing and should be eliminated. (See
“<a href="#Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos227519"
class="calibre18">Flag Arguments</a>” on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_045.html_filepos224416"
class="calibre18">41</a>.)

<span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_filepos1074468"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **F4: *Dead Function***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Methods that are never called should be discarded. Keeping dead code
around is wasteful. Don’t be afraid to delete the function. Remember,
your source code control system still remembers it.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_filepos1074875"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_157.html_calibre_pb_196" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html"></span>

### **General**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1075026"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G1: *Multiple Languages in One Source File***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Today’s modern programming environments make it possible to put many
different languages into a single source file. For example, a Java
source file might contain snippets of XML, HTML, YAML, JavaDoc, English,
JavaScript, and so on. For another example, in addition to HTML a JSP
file might contain Java, a tag library syntax, English comments,
Javadocs, XML, JavaScript, and so forth. This is confusing at best and
carelessly sloppy at worst.

The ideal is for a source file to contain one, and only one, language.
Realistically, we will probably have to use more than one. But we should
take pains to minimize both the number and extent of extra languages in
our source files.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1075999"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G2: *Obvious Behavior Is Unimplemented***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Following “The Principle of Least Surprise,”<sup>2</sup> any function or
class should implement the behaviors that another programmer could
reasonably expect. For example, consider a function that translates the
name of a day to an <span class="calibre28">`enum`</span> that
represents the day.

<span class="calibre34">2. Or “The Principle of Least Astonishment”:
<span class="calibre28">`http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/`  
`         Principle_of_least_astonishment`</span></span>

<span class="calibre28"><span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1076843"
class="calibre2"></span>   Day day = DayDate.StringToDay(String
dayName);</span>

We would expect the string “<span class="calibre28">`Monday`</span>” to
be translated to <span class="calibre28">`Day.MONDAY`</span>. We would
also expect the common abbreviations to be translated, and we would
expect the function to ignore case.

When an obvious behavior is not implemented, readers and users of the
code can no longer depend on their intuition about function names. They
lose their trust in the original author and must fall back on reading
the details of the code.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1077590"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G3: *Incorrect Behavior at the Boundaries***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It seems obvious to say that code should behave correctly. The problem
is that we seldom realize just how complicated correct behavior is.
Developers often write functions that they think will work, and then
trust their intuition rather than going to the effort to prove that
their code works in all the corner and boundary cases.

There is no replacement for due diligence. Every boundary condition,
every corner case, every quirk and exception represents something that
can confound an elegant and intuitive algorithm. *Don’t rely on your
intuition*. Look for every boundary condition and write a test for it.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1078503"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G4: *Overridden Safeties***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Chernobyl melted down because the plant manager overrode each of the
safety mechanisms one by one. The safeties were making it inconvenient
to run an experiment. The result was that the experiment did not get
run, and the world saw it’s first major civilian nuclear catastrophe.

It is risky to override safeties. Exerting manual control over
<span class="calibre28">`serialVersionUID`</span> may be necessary, but
it is always risky. Turning off certain compiler warnings (or all
warnings!) may help you get the build to succeed, but at the risk of
endless debugging sessions. Turning off failing tests and telling
yourself you’ll get them to pass later is as bad as pretending your
credit cards are free money.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1079501"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G5: *Duplication***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

This is one of the most important rules in this book, and you should
take it very seriously. Virtually every author who writes about software
design mentions this rule. Dave Thomas and Andy Hunt called it the
DRY<sup>3</sup> principle (Don’t Repeat Yourself). Kent Beck made it one
of the core principles of Extreme Programming and called it: “Once, and
only once.” Ron Jeffries ranks this rule second, just below getting all
the tests to pass.

<span class="calibre34">3.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1080191"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1176082"
class="calibre18">PRAG</a>\].</span>

Every time you see duplication in the code, it represents a missed
opportunity for abstraction. That duplication could probably become a
subroutine or perhaps another class outright. By folding the duplication
into such an abstraction, you increase the vocabulary of the language of
your design. Other programmers can use the abstract facilities
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1080641"
class="calibre2"></span>you create. Coding becomes faster and less error
prone because you have raised the abstraction level.

The most obvious form of duplication is when you have clumps of
identical code that look like some programmers went wild with the mouse,
pasting the same code over and over again. These should be replaced with
simple methods.

A more subtle form is the <span class="calibre28">`switch/case`</span>
or <span class="calibre28">`if/else`</span> chain that appears again and
again in various modules, always testing for the same set of conditions.
These should be replaced with polymorphism.

Still more subtle are the modules that have similar algorithms, but that
don’t share similar lines of code. This is still duplication and should
be addressed by using the T<span class="small">EMPLATE</span>
M<span class="small">ETHOD</span>,<sup>4</sup> or
S<span class="small">TRATEGY</span><sup>5</sup> pattern.

<span class="calibre34">4.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1081768"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1176337"
class="calibre18">GOF</a>\].</span>

<span class="calibre34">5.
\[<a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1176337"
class="calibre18">GOF</a>\].</span>

Indeed, most of the design patterns that have appeared in the last
fifteen years are simply well-known ways to eliminate duplication. So
too the Codd Normal Forms are a strategy for eliminating duplication in
database schemae. OO itself is a strategy for organizing modules and
eliminating duplication. Not surprisingly, so is structured programming.

I think the point has been made. Find and eliminate duplication wherever
you can.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1082531"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G6: *Code at Wrong Level of Abstraction***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It is important to create abstractions that separate higher level
general concepts from lower level detailed concepts. Sometimes we do
this by creating abstract classes to hold the higher level concepts and
derivatives to hold the lower level concepts. When we do this, we need
to make sure that the separation is complete. We want *all* the lower
level concepts to be in the derivatives and *all* the higher level
concepts to be in the base class.

For example, constants, variables, or utility functions that pertain
only to the detailed implementation should not be present in the base
class. The base class should know nothing about them.

This rule also pertains to source files, components, and modules. Good
software design requires that we separate concepts at different levels
and place them in different containers. Sometimes these containers are
base classes or derivatives and sometimes they are source files,
modules, or components. Whatever the case may be, the separation needs
to be complete. We don’t want lower and higher level concepts mixed
together.

Consider the following code:

<span class="calibre28">   public interface Stack {  
     Object pop() throws EmptyException;  
     void push(Object o) throws FullException;  
     double percentFull();  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1084312"
class="calibre2"></span>  
     class EmptyException extends Exception {}  
     class FullException extends Exception {}  
   }</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`percentFull`</span> function is at the
wrong level of abstraction. Although there are many implementations of
<span class="calibre28">`Stack`</span> where the concept of *fullness*
is reasonable, there are other implementations that simply *could not
know* how full they are. So the function would be better placed in a
derivative interface such as
<span class="calibre28">`BoundedStack`</span>.

Perhaps you are thinking that the implementation could just return zero
if the stack were boundless. The problem with that is that no stack is
truly boundless. You cannot really prevent an
<span class="calibre28">`OutOfMemoryException`</span> by checking for

<span class="calibre28">   stack.percentFull() \< 50.0.</span>

Implementing the function to return 0 would be telling a lie.

The point is that you cannot lie or fake your way out of a misplaced
abstraction. Isolating abstractions is one of the hardest things that
software developers do, and there is no quick fix when you get it wrong.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1085889"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G7: *Base Classes Depending on Their Derivatives***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The most common reason for partitioning concepts into base and
derivative classes is so that the higher level base class concepts can
be independent of the lower level derivative class concepts. Therefore,
when we see base classes mentioning the names of their derivatives, we
suspect a problem. In general, base classes should know nothing about
their derivatives.

There are exceptions to this rule, of course. Sometimes the number of
derivatives is strictly fixed, and the base class has code that selects
between the derivatives. We see this a lot in finite state machine
implementations. However, in that case the derivatives and base class
are strongly coupled and always deploy together in the same jar file. In
the general case we want to be able to deploy derivatives and bases in
different jar files.

Deploying derivatives and bases in different jar files and making sure
the base jar files know nothing about the contents of the derivative jar
files allow us to deploy our systems in discrete and independent
components. When such components are modified, they can be redeployed
without having to redeploy the base components. This means that the
impact of a change is greatly lessened, and maintaining systems in the
field is made much simpler.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1087497"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G8: *Too Much Information***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Well-defined modules have very small interfaces that allow you to do a
lot with a little. Poorly defined modules have wide and deep interfaces
that force you to use many different gestures to get simple things done.
A well-defined interface does not offer very many functions to depend
upon, so coupling is low. A poorly defined interface provides lots of
functions that you must call, so coupling is high.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1088121"
class="calibre2"></span>Good software developers learn to limit what
they expose at the interfaces of their classes and modules. The fewer
methods a class has, the better. The fewer variables a function knows
about, the better. The fewer instance variables a class has, the better.

Hide your data. Hide your utility functions. Hide your constants and
your temporaries. Don’t create classes with lots of methods or lots of
instance variables. Don’t create lots of protected variables and
functions for your subclasses. Concentrate on keeping interfaces very
tight and very small. Help keep coupling low by limiting information.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1088855"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G9: *Dead Code***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Dead code is code that isn’t executed. You find it in the body of an
<span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statement that checks for a
condition that can’t happen. You find it in the
<span class="calibre28">`catch`</span> block of a
<span class="calibre28">`try`</span> that never
<span class="calibre28">`throws`</span>. You find it in little utility
methods that are never called or
<span class="calibre28">`switch/case`</span> conditions that never
occur.

The problem with dead code is that after awhile it starts to smell. The
older it is, the stronger and sourer the odor becomes. This is because
dead code is not completely updated when designs change. It still
*compiles*, but it does not follow newer conventions or rules. It was
written at a time when the system was *different*. When you find dead
code, do the right thing. Give it a decent burial. Delete it from the
system.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1090037"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G10: *Vertical Separation***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Variables and function should be defined close to where they are used.
Local variables should be declared just above their first usage and
should have a small vertical scope. We don’t want local variables
declared hundreds of lines distant from their usages.

Private functions should be defined just below their first usage.
Private functions belong to the scope of the whole class, but we’d still
like to limit the vertical distance between the invocations and
definitions. Finding a private function should just be a matter of
scanning downward from the first usage.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1090891"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G11: *Inconsistency***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

If you do something a certain way, do all similar things in the same
way. This goes back to the principle of least surprise. Be careful with
the conventions you choose, and once chosen, be careful to continue to
follow them.

If within a particular function you use a variable named
<span class="calibre28">`response`</span> to hold an
<span class="calibre28">`HttpServletResponse`</span>, then use the same
variable name consistently in the other functions that use
<span class="calibre28">`HttpServletResponse`</span> objects. If you
name a method
<span class="calibre28">`processVerificationRequest`</span>, then use a
similar name, such as
<span class="calibre28">`processDeletionRequest`</span>, for the methods
that process other kinds of requests.

Simple consistency like this, when reliably applied, can make code much
easier to read and modify.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1092077"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1092130" class="calibre2"></span>**G12: *Clutter***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Of what use is a default constructor with no implementation? All it
serves to do is clutter up the code with meaningless artifacts.
Variables that aren’t used, functions that are never called, comments
that add no information, and so forth. All these things are clutter and
should be removed. Keep your source files clean, well organized, and
free of clutter.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1092655"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G13: *Artificial Coupling***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Things that don’t depend upon each other should not be artificially
coupled. For example, general <span class="calibre28">`enums`</span>
should not be contained within more specific classes because this forces
the whole application to know about these more specific classes. The
same goes for general purpose <span class="calibre28">`static`</span>
functions being declared in specific classes.

In general an artificial coupling is a coupling between two modules that
serves no direct purpose. It is a result of putting a variable,
constant, or function in a temporarily convenient, though inappropriate,
location. This is lazy and careless.

Take the time to figure out where functions, constants, and variables
ought to be declared. Don’t just toss them in the most convenient place
at hand and then leave them there.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1093819"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G14: *Feature Envy***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

This is one of Martin Fowler’s code smells.<sup>6</sup> The methods of a
class should be interested in the variables and functions of the class
they belong to, and not the variables and functions of other classes.
When a method uses accessors and mutators of some other object to
manipulate the data within that object, then it *envies* the scope of
the class of that other object. It wishes that it were inside that other
class so that it could have direct access to the variables it is
manipulating. For example:

<span class="calibre34">6.
\[<a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1175798"
class="calibre18">Refactoring</a>\].</span>

<span class="calibre28">   public class HourlyPayCalculator {  
     public Money calculateWeeklyPay(HourlyEmployee e) {  
       int tenthRate = e.getTenthRate().getPennies();  
       int tenthsWorked = e.getTenthsWorked();  
       int straightTime = Math.min(400, tenthsWorked);  
       int overTime = Math.max(0, tenthsWorked - straightTime);  
       int straightPay = straightTime \* tenthRate;  
       int overtimePay = (int)Math.round(overTime\*tenthRate\*1.5);  
       return new Money(straightPay + overtimePay);  
     }  
   }</span>

The <span class="calibre28">`calculateWeeklyPay`</span> method reaches
into the <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span> object to get
the data on which it operates. The
