<span class="calibre28">`calculateWeeklyPay`</span> method *envies* the
scope of <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span>. It “wishes”
that it could be inside <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1096096"
class="calibre2"></span>All else being equal, we want to eliminate
Feature Envy because it exposes the internals of one class to another.
Sometimes, however, Feature Envy is a necessary evil. Consider the
following:

<span class="calibre28">   public class HourlyEmployeeReport {  
     private HourlyEmployee employee ;  
  
     public HourlyEmployeeReport(HourlyEmployee e) {  
       this.employee = e;  
     }  
  
     String reportHours() {  
       return String.format(  
         “Name: %s\tHours:%d.%1d\n”,  
         employee.getName(),  
         employee.getTenthsWorked()/10,  
         employee.getTenthsWorked()%10);  
     }  
   }</span>

Clearly, the <span class="calibre28">`reportHours`</span> method envies
the <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span> class. On the other
hand, we don’t want <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span> to
have to know about the format of the report. Moving that format string
into the <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span> class would
violate several principles of object oriented design.<sup>7</sup> It
would couple <span class="calibre28">`HourlyEmployee`</span> to the
format of the report, exposing it to changes in that format.

<span class="calibre34">7. Specifically, the Single Responsibility
Principle, the Open Closed Principle, and the Common Closure Principle.
See \[<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1098046"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1177107"
class="calibre18">PPP</a>\].</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1098149"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G15: *Selector Arguments***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

There is hardly anything more abominable than a dangling
<span class="calibre28">`false`</span> argument at the end of a function
call. What does it mean? What would it change if it were
<span class="calibre28">`true`</span>? Not only is the purpose of a
selector argument difficult to remember, each selector argument combines
many functions into one. Selector arguments are just a lazy way to avoid
splitting a large function into several smaller functions. Consider:

<span class="calibre28">   public int calculateWeeklyPay(boolean
overtime) {  
     int tenthRate = getTenthRate();  
     int tenthsWorked = getTenthsWorked();  
     int straightTime = Math.min(400, tenthsWorked);  
     int overTime = Math.max(0, tenthsWorked - straightTime);  
     int straightPay = straightTime \* tenthRate;  
     double overtimeRate = overtime ? 1.5 : 1.0 \* tenthRate;  
     int overtimePay = (int)Math.round(overTime\*overtimeRate);  
     return straightPay + overtimePay;  
   }</span>

You call this function with a <span class="calibre28">`true`</span> if
overtime is paid as time and a half, and with a
<span class="calibre28">`false`</span> if overtime is paid as straight
time. It’s bad enough that you must remember what
<span class="calibre28">`calculateWeeklyPay(false)`</span> means
whenever you happen to stumble across it. But the
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1100015"
class="calibre2"></span>real shame of a function like this is that the
author missed the opportunity to write the following:

<span class="calibre28">   public int straightPay() {  
     return getTenthsWorked() \* getTenthRate();  
   }  
  
   public int overTimePay() {  
     int overTimeTenths = Math.max(0, getTenthsWorked() - 400);  
     int overTimePay = overTimeBonus(overTimeTenths);  
     return straightPay() + overTimePay;  
   }  
  
   private int overTimeBonus(int overTimeTenths) {  
     double bonus = 0.5 \* getTenthRate() \* overTimeTenths;  
     return (int) Math.round(bonus);  
   }</span>

Of course, selectors need not be
<span class="calibre28">`boolean`</span>. They can be enums, integers,
or any other type of argument that is used to select the behavior of the
function. In general it is better to have many functions than to pass
some code into a function to select the behavior.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1101337"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G16: *Obscured Intent***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

We want code to be as expressive as possible. Run-on expressions,
Hungarian notation, and magic numbers all obscure the author’s intent.
For example, here is the <span class="calibre28">`overTimePay`</span>
function as it might have appeared:

<span class="calibre28">   public int m_otCalc() {  
   return iThsWkd \* iThsRte +  
     (int) Math.round(0.5 \* iThsRte \*  
       Math.max(0, iThsWkd - 400)  
     );  
   }</span>

Small and dense as this might appear, it’s also virtually impenetrable.
It is worth taking the time to make the intent of our code visible to
our readers.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1102388"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G17: *Misplaced Responsibility***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

One of the most important decisions a software developer can make is
where to put code. For example, where should the
<span class="calibre28">`PI`</span> constant go? Should it be in the
<span class="calibre28">`Math`</span> class? Perhaps it belongs in the
<span class="calibre28">`Trigonometry`</span> class? Or maybe in the
<span class="calibre28">`Circle`</span> class?

The principle of least surprise comes into play here. Code should be
placed where a reader would naturally expect it to be. The
<span class="calibre28">`PI`</span> constant should go where the trig
functions are declared. The OVERTIME_RATE constant should be declared in
the <span class="calibre28">`HourlyPay-Calculator`</span> class.

Sometimes we get “clever” about where to put certain functionality.
We’ll put it in a function that’s convenient for us, but not necessarily
intuitive to the reader. For example, perhaps we need to print a report
with the total of hours that an employee worked. We
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1103677"
class="calibre2"></span>could sum up those hours in the code that prints
the report, or we could try to keep a running total in the code that
accepts time cards.

One way to make this decision is to look at the names of the functions.
Let’s say that our report module has a function named
<span class="calibre28">`getTotalHours`</span>. Let’s also say that the
module that accepts time cards has a
<span class="calibre28">`saveTimeCard`</span> function. Which of these
two functions, by it’s name, implies that it calculates the total? The
answer should be obvious.

Clearly, there are sometimes performance reasons why the total should be
calculated as time cards are accepted rather than when the report is
printed. That’s fine, but the names of the functions ought to reflect
this. For example, there should be a
<span class="calibre28">`computeRunning-TotalOfHours`</span> function in
the timecard module.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1104766"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G18: *Inappropriate Static***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">`Math.max(double a, double b)`</span> is a good
static method. It does not operate on a single instance; indeed, it
would be silly to have to say
<span class="calibre28">`new Math().max(a,b)`</span> or even
<span class="calibre28">`a.max(b)`</span>. All the data that
<span class="calibre28">`max`</span> uses comes from its two arguments,
and not from any “owning” object. More to the point, there is almost *no
chance* that we’d want <span class="calibre28">`Math.max`</span> to be
polymorphic.

Sometimes, however, we write static functions that should not be static.
For example, consider:

<span class="calibre28">   HourlyPayCalculator.calculatePay(employee,
overtimeRate).</span>

Again, this seems like a reasonable
<span class="calibre28">`static`</span> function. It doesn’t operate on
any particular object and gets all it’s data from it’s arguments.
However, there is a reasonable chance that we’ll want this function to
be polymorphic. We may wish to implement several different algorithms
for calculating hourly pay, for example,
<span class="calibre28">`OvertimeHourlyPayCalculator`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`StraightTimeHourlyPayCalculator`</span>. So in
this case the function should not be static. It should be a nonstatic
member function of <span class="calibre28">`Employee`</span>.

In general you should prefer nonstatic methods to static methods. When
in doubt, make the function nonstatic. If you really want a function to
be static, make sure that there is no chance that you’ll want it to
behave polymorphically.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1106856"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G19: *Use Explanatory Variables***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Kent Beck wrote about this in his great book *Smalltalk Best Practice
Patterns*<sup>8</sup> and again more recently in his equally great book
*Implementation Patterns*.<sup>9</sup> One of the more powerful ways to
make a program readable is to break the calculations up into
intermediate values that are held in variables with meaningful names.

<span class="calibre34">8.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1107457"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1176617"
class="calibre18">Beck97</a>\], p.
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_079.html_filepos444187"
class="calibre18">108</a>.</span>

<span class="calibre34">9.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1107617"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1176866"
class="calibre18">Beck07</a>\].</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1107723"
class="calibre2"></span>Consider this example from FitNesse:

<span class="calibre28">   Matcher match =
headerPattern.matcher(line);  
   if(match.find())  
   {  
     String key = match.group(1);  
     String value = match.group(2);  
     headers.put(key.toLowerCase(), value);  
   }</span>

The simple use of explanatory variables makes it clear that the first
matched group is the *key*, and the second matched group is the *value*.

It is hard to overdo this. More explanatory variables are generally
better than fewer. It is remarkable how an opaque module can suddenly
become transparent simply by breaking the calculations up into
well-named intermediate values.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1108754"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G20: *Function Names Should Say What They Do***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Look at this code:

<span class="calibre28">   Date newDate = date.add(5);</span>

Would you expect this to add five days to the date? Or is it weeks, or
hours? Is the <span class="calibre28">`date`</span> instance changed or
does the function just return a new
<span class="calibre28">`Date`</span> without changing the old one? *You
can’t tell from the call what the function does*.

If the function adds five days to the date and changes the date, then it
should be called <span class="calibre28">`addDaysTo`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`increaseByDays`</span>. If, on the other hand,
the function returns a new date that is five days later but does not
change the date instance, it should be called
<span class="calibre28">`daysLater`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`daysSince`</span>.

If you have to look at the implementation (or documentation) of the
function to know what it does, then you should work to find a better
name or rearrange the functionality so that it can be placed in
functions with better names.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1110271"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G21: *Understand the Algorithm***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Lots of very funny code is written because people don’t take the time to
understand the algorithm. They get something to work by plugging in
enough <span class="calibre28">`if`</span> statements and flags, without
really stopping to consider what is really going on.

Programming is often an exploration. You *think* you know the right
algorithm for something, but then you wind up fiddling with it, prodding
and poking at it, until you get it to “work.” How do you know it
“works”? Because it passes the test cases you can think of.

There is nothing wrong with this approach. Indeed, often it is the only
way to get a function to do what you think it should. However, it is not
sufficient to leave the quotation marks around the word “work.”

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1111398"
class="calibre2"></span>Before you consider yourself to be done with a
function, make sure you *understand* how it works. It is not good enough
that it passes all the tests. You must *know*<sup>10</sup> that the
solution is correct.

<span class="calibre34">10. There is a difference between knowing how
the code works and knowing whether the algorithm will do the job
required of it. Being unsure that an algorithm is appropriate is often a
fact of life. Being unsure what your code does is just laziness.</span>

Often the best way to gain this knowledge and understanding is to
refactor the function into something that is so clean and expressive
that it is *obvious* how it works.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1112244"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G22: *Make Logical Dependencies Physical***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

If one module depends upon another, that dependency should be physical,
not just logical. The dependent module should not make assumptions (in
other words, logical dependencies) about the module it depends upon.
Rather it should explicitly ask that module for all the information it
depends upon.

For example, imagine that you are writing a function that prints a plain
text report of hours worked by employees. One class named
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReporter`</span> gathers all the data
into a convenient form and then passes it to
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReportFormatter`</span> to print it. (See
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1113193"
class="calibre18">Listing 17-1</a>.)

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1113193"
class="calibre2"></span>

<span class="calibre5">**Listing 17-1
<span class="calibre37">`HourlyReporter.java`</span>**</span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span class="calibre28">   public class HourlyReporter {  
     private HourlyReportFormatter formatter;  
     private List\<LineItem\> page;  
     private final int PAGE_SIZE = 55;  
  
     public HourlyReporter(HourlyReportFormatter formatter) {  
       this.formatter = formatter;  
       page = new ArrayList\<LineItem\>();  
     }  
  
     public void generateReport(List\<HourlyEmployee\> employees) {  
       for (HourlyEmployee e : employees) {  
         addLineItemToPage(e);  
         if (page.size() == PAGE_SIZE)  
           printAndClearItemList();  
       }  
       if (page.size() \> 0)  
         printAndClearItemList();  
     }  
  
     private void printAndClearItemList() {  
       formatter.format(page);  
       page.clear();  
     }  
  
     private void addLineItemToPage(HourlyEmployee e) {  
       LineItem item = new LineItem();  
       item.name = e.getName();  
       item.hours = e.getTenthsWorked() / 10;  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1115252"
class="calibre2"></span>  
       item.tenths = e.getTenthsWorked() % 10;  
       page.add(item);  
     }  
  
     public class LineItem {  
       public String name;  
       public int hours;  
       public int tenths;  
     }  
   }</span>

This code has a logical dependency that has not been physicalized. Can
you spot it? It is the constant
<span class="calibre28">`PAGE_SIZE`</span>. Why should the
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReporter`</span> know the size of the
page? Page size should be the responsibility of the
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReportFormatter`</span>.

The fact that PAGE_SIZE is declared in
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReporter`</span> represents a misplaced
responsibility \[G17\] that causes
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReporter`</span> to assume that it knows
what the page size ought to be. Such an assumption is a logical
dependency. <span class="calibre28">`HourlyReporter`</span> depends on
the fact that <span class="calibre28">`HourlyReportFormatter`</span> can
deal with page sizes of 55. If some implementation of
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReportFormatter`</span> could not deal
with such sizes, then there would be an error.

We can physicalize this dependency by creating a new method in
<span class="calibre28">`HourlyReport-Formatter`</span> named
<span class="calibre28">`getMaxPageSize(). HourlyReporter`</span> will
then call that function rather than using the
<span class="calibre28">`PAGE_SIZE`</span> constant.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1117244"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G23: *Prefer Polymorphism to If/Else or Switch/Case***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

This might seem a strange suggestion given the topic of
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_067.html_filepos398983"
class="calibre18">Chapter 6</a>. After all, in that chapter I make the
point that switch statements are probably appropriate in the parts of
the system where adding new functions is more likely than adding new
types.

First, most people use switch statements because it’s the obvious brute
force solution, not because it’s the right solution for the situation.
So this heuristic is here to remind us to consider polymorphism before
using a switch.

Second, the cases where functions are more volatile than types are
relatively rare. So *every* switch statement should be suspect.

I use the following “O<span class="small">NE</span>
S<span class="small">WITCH</span>” rule: *There may be no more than one
switch statement for a given type of selection. The cases in that switch
statement must create polymorphic objects that take the place of other
such switch statements in the rest of the system.*

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1118627"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G24: *Follow Standard Conventions***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Every team should follow a coding standard based on common industry
norms. This coding standard should specify things like where to declare
instance variables; how to name classes, methods, and variables; where
to put braces; and so on. The team should not need a document to
describe these conventions because their code provides the examples.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1119197"
class="calibre2"></span>Everyone on the team should follow these
conventions. This means that each team member must be mature enough to
realize that it doesn’t matter a whit where you put your braces so long
as you all agree on where to put them.

If you would like to know what conventions I follow, you’ll see them in
the refactored code in
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1295173"
class="calibre18">Listing B-7</a> on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1295252"
class="calibre18">394</a>, through
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_175.html_filepos1298530"
class="calibre18">Listing B-14</a>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1119774"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G25: *Replace Magic Numbers with Named Constants***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

This is probably one of the oldest rules in software development. I
remember reading it in the late sixties in introductory COBOL, FORTRAN,
and PL/1 manuals. In general it is a bad idea to have raw numbers in
your code. You should hide them behind well-named constants.

For example, the number 86,400 should be hidden behind the constant
<span class="calibre28">`SECONDS_PER_DAY`</span>. If you are printing 55
lines per page, then the constant 55 should be hidden behind the
constant <span class="calibre28">`LINES_PER_PAGE`</span>.

Some constants are so easy to recognize that they don’t always need a
named constant to hide behind so long as they are used in conjunction
with very self-explanatory code. For example:

<span class="calibre28">   double milesWalked = feetWalked/5280.0;  
   int dailyPay = hourlyRate \* 8;  
   double circumference = radius \* Math.PI \* 2;</span>

Do we really need the constants
<span class="calibre28">`FEET_PER_MILE, WORK_HOURS_PER_DAY`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`TWO`</span> in the above examples? Clearly, the
last case is absurd. There are some formulae in which constants are
simply better written as raw numbers. You might quibble about the
<span class="calibre28">`WORK_HOURS_PER_DAY`</span> case because the
laws or conventions might change. On the other hand, that formula reads
so nicely with the 8 in it that I would be reluctant to add 17 extra
characters to the readers’ burden. And in the
<span class="calibre28">`FEET_PER_MILE`</span> case, the number 5280 is
so very well known and so unique a constant that readers would recognize
it even if it stood alone on a page with no context surrounding it.

Constants like 3.141592653589793 are also very well known and easily
recognizable. However, the chance for error is too great to leave them
raw. Every time someone sees 3.1415927535890793, they know that it is
*π*, and so they fail to scrutinize it. (Did you catch the single-digit
error?) We also don’t want people using 3.14, 3.14159, 3.142, and so
forth. Therefore, it is a good thing that
<span class="calibre28">`Math.PI`</span> has already been defined for
us.

The term “Magic Number” does not apply only to numbers. It applies to
any token that has a value that is not self-describing. For example:

<span class="calibre28">   assertEquals(7777, Employee.find(“John
Doe”).employeeNumber());</span>

There are two magic numbers in this assertion. The first is obviously
7777, though what it might mean is not obvious. The second magic number
is “<span class="calibre28">`John Doe`</span>,” and again the intent is
not clear.

It turns out that “<span class="calibre28">`John Doe`</span>” is the
name of employee \#7777 in a well-known test database created by our
team. Everyone in the team knows that when you connect to this
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1123393"
class="calibre2"></span>database, it will have several employees already
cooked into it with well-known values and attributes. It also turns out
that “<span class="calibre28">`John Doe`</span>” represents the sole
hourly employee in that test database. So this test should really read:

<span class="calibre28">   assertEquals(  
     HOURLY_EMPLOYEE_ID,  
     Employee.find(HOURLY_EMPLOYEE_NAME).employeeNumber());</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1123985"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G26: *Be Precise***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Expecting the first match to be the *only* match to a query is probably
naive. Using floating point numbers to represent currency is almost
criminal. Avoiding locks and/or transaction management because you don’t
think concurrent update is likely is lazy at best. Declaring a variable
to be an <span class="calibre28">`ArrayList`</span> when a
<span class="calibre28">`List`</span> will due is overly constraining.
Making all variables <span class="calibre28">`protected`</span> by
default is not constraining enough.

When you make a decision in your code, make sure you make it
*precisely*. Know why you have made it and how you will deal with any
exceptions. Don’t be lazy about the precision of your decisions. If you
decide to call a function that might return
<span class="calibre28">`null`</span>, make sure you check for
<span class="calibre28">`null`</span>. If you query for what you think
is the only record in the database, make sure your code checks to be
sure there aren’t others. If you need to deal with currency, use
integers<sup>11</sup> and deal with rounding appropriately. If there is
the possibility of concurrent update, make sure you implement some kind
of locking mechanism.

<span class="calibre34">11. Or better yet, a
<span class="calibre28">`Money`</span> class that uses integers.</span>

Ambiguities and imprecision in code are either a result of disagreements
or laziness. In either case they should be eliminated.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1125840"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G27: *Structure over Convention***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Enforce design decisions with structure over convention. Naming
conventions are good, but they are inferior to structures that force
compliance. For example, switch/cases with nicely named enumerations are
inferior to base classes with abstract methods. No one is forced to
implement the <span class="calibre28">`switch/case`</span> statement the
same way each time; but the base classes do enforce that concrete
classes have all abstract methods implemented.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1126525"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G28: *Encapsulate Conditionals***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Boolean logic is hard enough to understand without having to see it in
the context of an <span class="calibre28">`if`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`while`</span> statement. Extract functions that
explain the intent of the conditional.

For example:

<span class="calibre28">   if (shouldBeDeleted(timer))</span>

is preferable to

<span class="calibre28">   if (timer.hasExpired() &&
!timer.isRecurrent())</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1127409"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1127462" class="calibre2"></span>**G29: *Avoid Negative Conditionals***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Negatives are just a bit harder to understand than positives. So, when
possible, conditionals should be expressed as positives. For example:

<span class="calibre28">   if (buffer.shouldCompact())</span>

is preferable to

<span class="calibre28">   if (!buffer.shouldNotCompact())</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1128107"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G30: *Functions Should Do One Thing***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

It is often tempting to create functions that have multiple sections
that perform a series of operations. Functions of this kind do more than
*one thing*, and should be converted into many smaller functions, each
of which does *one thing*.

For example:

<span class="calibre28">   public void pay() {  
     for (Employee e : employees) {  
       if (e.isPayday()) {  
         Money pay = e.calculatePay();  
         e.deliverPay(pay);  
       }  
     }  
   }</span>

This bit of code does three things. It loops over all the employees,
checks to see whether each employee ought to be paid, and then pays the
employee. This code would be better written as:

<span class="calibre28">   public void pay() {  
     for (Employee e : employees)  
       payIfNecessary(e);  
   }  
  
   private void payIfNecessary(Employee e) {  
     if (e.isPayday())  
       calculateAndDeliverPay(e);  
   }  
  
   private void calculateAndDeliverPay(Employee e) {  
     Money pay = e.calculatePay();  
     e.deliverPay(pay);  
   }</span>

Each of these functions does one thing. (See
“<a href="#Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos202749" class="calibre18">Do
One Thing</a>” on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_040.html_filepos200768"
class="calibre18">35</a>.)

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1130323"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G31: *Hidden Temporal Couplings***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Temporal couplings are often necessary, but you should not hide the
coupling. Structure the arguments of your functions such that the order
in which they should be called is obvious. Consider the following:

<span class="calibre28"><span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1130768"
class="calibre2"></span>   public class MoogDiver {  
     Gradient gradient;  
     List\<Spline\> splines;  
  
     public void dive(String reason) {  
       saturateGradient();  
       reticulateSplines();  
       diveForMoog(reason);  
     }  
     …  
   }</span>

The order of the three functions is important. You must saturate the
gradient before you can reticulate the splines, and only then can you
dive for the moog. Unfortunately, the code does not enforce this
temporal coupling. Another programmer could call
<span class="calibre28">`reticulate-Splines`</span> before
<span class="calibre28">`saturateGradient`</span> was called, leading to
an <span class="calibre28">`UnsaturatedGradientException`</span>. A
better solution is:

<span class="calibre28">   public class MoogDiver {  
     Gradient gradient;  
     List\<Spline\> splines;  
  
     public void dive(String reason) {  
       Gradient gradient = saturateGradient();  
       List\<Spline\> splines = reticulateSplines(gradient);  
       diveForMoog(splines, reason);  
     }  
     …  
   }</span>

This exposes the temporal coupling by creating a bucket brigade. Each
function produces a result that the next function needs, so there is no
reasonable way to call them out of order.

You might complain that this increases the complexity of the functions,
and you’d be right. But that extra syntactic complexity exposes the true
temporal complexity of the situation.

Note that I left the instance variables in place. I presume that they
are needed by private methods in the class. Even so, I want the
arguments in place to make the temporal coupling explicit.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1133319"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G32: *Don’t Be Arbitrary***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Have a reason for the way you structure your code, and make sure that
reason is communicated by the structure of the code. If a structure
appears arbitrary, others will feel empowered to change it. If a
structure appears consistently throughout the system, others will use it
and preserve the convention. For example, I was recently merging changes
to FitNesse and discovered that one of our committers had done this:

<span class="calibre28">   public class AliasLinkWidget extends
ParentWidget  
   {  
     public static class VariableExpandingWidgetRoot {  
       …  
  
     …  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1134339"
class="calibre2"></span>The problem with this was that
<span class="calibre28">`VariableExpandingWidgetRoot`</span> had no need
to be inside the scope of
<span class="calibre28">`AliasLinkWidget`</span>. Moreover, other
unrelated classes made use of
<span class="calibre28">`AliasLinkWidget.VariableExpandingWidgetRoot`</span>.
These classes had no need to know about
<span class="calibre28">`AliasLinkWidget`</span>.

Perhaps the programmer had plopped the
<span class="calibre28">`VariableExpandingWidgetRoot`</span> into
<span class="calibre28">`AliasWidget`</span> as a matter of convenience,
or perhaps he thought it really needed to be scoped inside
<span class="calibre28">`AliasWidget`</span>. Whatever the reason, the
result wound up being arbitrary. Public classes that are not utilities
of some other class should not be scoped inside another class. The
convention is to make them public at the top level of their package.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1135381"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G33: *Encapsulate Boundary Conditions***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Boundary conditions are hard to keep track of. Put the processing for
them in one place. Don’t let them leak all over the code. We don’t want
swarms of <span class="calibre28">`+1`</span>s and
<span class="calibre28">`-1`</span>s scattered hither and yon. Consider
this simple example from FIT:

<span class="calibre28">   if(level + 1 \< tags.length)  
   {  
     parts = new Parse(body, tags, level + 1, offset + endTag);  
     body = null;  
   }</span>

Notice that <span class="calibre28">`level+1`</span> appears twice. This
is a boundary condition that should be encapsulated within a variable
named something like <span class="calibre28">`nextLevel`</span>.

<span class="calibre28">   int nextLevel = level + 1;  
   if(nextLevel \< tags.length)  
   {  
     parts = new Parse(body, tags, nextLevel, offset + endTag);  
     body = null;  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1136868"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G34: *Functions Should Descend Only One Level of Abstraction***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The statements within a function should all be written at the same level
of abstraction, which should be one level below the operation described
by the name of the function. This may be the hardest of these heuristics
to interpret and follow. Though the idea is plain enough, humans are
just far too good at seamlessly mixing levels of abstraction. Consider,
for example, the following code taken from FitNesse:

<span class="calibre28">   public String render() throws Exception  
   {  
     StringBuffer html = new StringBuffer(“\<hr”);  
     if(size \> 0)  
       html.append(” size=\\”).append(size + 1).append(”\\”);  
     html.append(“\>”);  
  
     return html.toString();  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1138128"
class="calibre2"></span>A moment’s study and you can see what’s going
on. This function constructs the HTML tag that draws a horizontal rule
across the page. The height of that rule is specified in the
<span class="calibre28">`size`</span> variable.

Now look again. This method is mixing at least two levels of
abstraction. The first is the notion that a horizontal rule has a size.
The second is the syntax of the <span class="calibre28">`HR`</span> tag
itself. This code comes from the
<span class="calibre28">`HruleWidget`</span> module in FitNesse. This
module detects a row of four or more dashes and converts it into the
appropriate HR tag. The more dashes, the larger the size.

I refactored this bit of code as follows. Note that I changed the name
of the <span class="calibre28">`size`</span> field to reflect its true
purpose. It held the number of extra dashes.

<span class="calibre28">   public String render() throws Exception  
   {  
     HtmlTag hr = new HtmlTag(“hr”);  
     if (extraDashes \> 0)  
       hr.addAttribute(“size”, hrSize(extraDashes));  
     return hr.html();  
   }  
  
   private String hrSize(int height)  
   {  
     int hrSize = height + 1;  
     return String.format(“%d”, hrSize);  
   }</span>

This change separates the two levels of abstraction nicely. The
<span class="calibre28">`render`</span> function simply constructs an HR
tag, without having to know anything about the HTML syntax of that tag.
The <span class="calibre28">`HtmlTag`</span> module takes care of all
the nasty syntax issues.

Indeed, by making this change I caught a subtle error. The original code
did not put the closing slash on the HR tag, as the XHTML standard would
have it. (In other words, it emitted
<span class="calibre28">`<hr>`</span> instead of
<span class="calibre28">`<hr/>`</span>.) The
<span class="calibre28">`HtmlTag`</span> module had been changed to
conform to XHTML long ago.

Separating levels of abstraction is one of the most important functions
of refactoring, and it’s one of the hardest to do well. As an example,
look at the code below. This was my first attempt at separating the
abstraction levels in the
<span class="calibre28">`HruleWidget.render method`</span>.

<span class="calibre28">   public String render() throws Exception  
   {  
     HtmlTag hr = new HtmlTag(“hr”);  
     if (size \> 0) {  
       hr.addAttribute(“size”, “”+(size+1));  
     }  
     return hr.html();  
   }</span>

My goal, at this point, was to create the necessary separation and get
the tests to pass. I accomplished that goal easily, but the result was a
function that *still* had mixed levels of abstraction. In this case the
mixed levels were the construction of the HR tag and the
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1141860"
class="calibre2"></span>interpretation and formatting of the
<span class="calibre28">`size`</span> variable. This points out that
when you break a function along lines of abstraction, you often uncover
new lines of abstraction that were obscured by the previous structure.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1142174"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G35: *Keep Configurable Data at High Levels***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

If you have a constant such as a default or configuration value that is
known and expected at a high level of abstraction, do not bury it in a
low-level function. Expose it as an argument to that low-level function
called from the high-level function. Consider the following code from
FitNesse:

<span class="calibre28">     public static void main(String\[\] args)
throws Exception  
     {  
       Arguments arguments = parseCommandLine(args);  
       …  
     }  
  
   public class Arguments  
   {  
     public static final String DEFAULT_PATH = “.”;  
     public static final String DEFAULT_ROOT = “FitNesseRoot”;  
     public static final int DEFAULT_PORT = 80;  
     public static final int DEFAULT_VERSION_DAYS = 14;  
     …  
   }</span>

The command-line arguments are parsed in the very first executable line
of FitNesse. The default values of those arguments are specified at the
top of the <span class="calibre28">`Argument`</span> class. You don’t
have to go looking in low levels of the system for statements like this
one:

<span class="calibre28">   if (arguments.port == 0) // use 80 by
default</span>

The configuration constants reside at a very high level and are easy to
change. They get passed down to the rest of the application. The lower
levels of the application do not own the values of these constants.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1144350"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **G36: *Avoid Transitive Navigation***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

In general we don’t want a single module to know much about its
collaborators. More specifically, if <span class="calibre28">`A`</span>
collaborates with <span class="calibre28">`B`</span>, and
<span class="calibre28">`B`</span> collaborates with
<span class="calibre28">`C`</span>, we don’t want modules that use
<span class="calibre28">`A`</span> to know about
<span class="calibre28">`C`</span>. (For example, we don’t want
<span class="calibre28">`a.getB().getC().doSomething()`</span>;.)

This is sometimes called the Law of Demeter. The Pragmatic Programmers
call it “Writing Shy Code.”<sup>12</sup> In either case it comes down to
making sure that modules know only about their immediate collaborators
and do not know the navigation map of the whole system.

<span class="calibre34">12.
\[<a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1176082"
class="calibre18">PRAG</a>\], p.
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_103.html_filepos540690"
class="calibre18">138</a>.</span>

If many modules used some form of the statement
<span class="calibre28">`a.getB().getC()`</span>, then it would be
difficult to change the design and architecture to interpose a
<span class="calibre28">`Q`</span> between
<span class="calibre28">`B`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`C`</span>. You’d
<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1145907"
class="calibre2"></span>have to find every instance of
<span class="calibre28">`a.getB().getC()`</span> and convert it to
<span class="calibre28">`a.getB().getQ().getC()`</span>. This is how
architectures become rigid. Too many modules know too much about the
architecture.

Rather we want our immediate collaborators to offer all the services we
need. We should not have to roam through the object graph of the system,
hunting for the method we want to call. Rather we should simply be able
to say:

<span class="calibre28">   myCollaborator.doSomething().</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_filepos1146631"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_158.html_calibre_pb_197" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html"></span>

### **Java**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_filepos1146779"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **J1: *Avoid Long Import Lists by Using Wildcards***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

If you use two or more classes from a package, then import the whole
package with

<span class="calibre28">   import package.\*;</span>

Long lists of imports are daunting to the reader. We don’t want to
clutter up the tops of our modules with 80 lines of imports. Rather we
want the imports to be a concise statement about which packages we
collaborate with.

Specific imports are hard dependencies, whereas wildcard imports are
not. If you specifically import a class, then that class *must* exist.
But if you import a package with a wildcard, no particular classes need
to exist. The import statement simply adds the package to the search
path when hunting for names. So no true dependency is created by such
imports, and they therefore serve to keep our modules less coupled.

There are times when the long list of specific imports can be useful.
For example, if you are dealing with legacy code and you want to find
out what classes you need to build mocks and stubs for, you can walk
down the list of specific imports to find out the true qualified names
of all those classes and then put the appropriate stubs in place.
However, this use for specific imports is very rare. Furthermore, most
modern IDEs will allow you to convert the wildcarded imports to a list
of specific imports with a single command. So even in the legacy case
it’s better to import wildcards.

Wildcard imports can sometimes cause name conflicts and ambiguities. Two
classes with the same name, but in different packages, will need to be
specifically imported, or at least specifically qualified when used.
This can be a nuisance but is rare enough that using wildcard imports is
still generally better than specific imports.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_filepos1149022"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **J2: *Don’t Inherit Constants***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

I have seen this several times and it always makes me grimace. A
programmer puts some constants in an interface and then gains access to
those constants by inheriting that interface. Take a look at the
following code:

<span class="calibre28">   public class HourlyEmployee extends Employee
{  
     private int tenthsWorked;  
     private double hourlyRate;  
<span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_filepos1149671"
class="calibre2"></span>  
     public Money calculatePay() {  
       int straightTime = Math.min(tenthsWorked, TENTHS_PER_WEEK);  
       int overTime = tenthsWorked - straightTime;  
       return new Money(  
         hourlyRate \* (tenthsWorked + OVERTIME_RATE \* overTime)  
       );  
     }  
     …  
   }</span>

Where did the constants <span class="calibre28">`TENTHS_PER_WEEK`</span>
and <span class="calibre28">`OVERTIME_RATE`</span> come from? They might
have come from class <span class="calibre28">`Employee`</span>; so let’s
take a look at that:

<span class="calibre28">   public abstract class Employee implements
PayrollConstants {  
     public abstract boolean isPayday();  
     public abstract Money calculatePay();  
     public abstract void deliverPay(Money pay);  
   }</span>

Nope, not there. But then where? Look closely at class
<span class="calibre28">`Employee`</span>. It implements
<span class="calibre28">`PayrollConstants`</span>.

<span class="calibre28">   public interface PayrollConstants {  
     public static final int TENTHS_PER_WEEK = 400;  
     public static final double OVERTIME_RATE = 1.5;  
   }</span>

This is a hideous practice! The constants are hidden at the top of the
inheritance hierarchy. Ick! Don’t use inheritance as a way to cheat the
scoping rules of the language. Use a static import instead.

<span class="calibre28">   **import static PayrollConstants.\*;**  
  
   public class HourlyEmployee extends Employee {  
     private int tenthsWorked;  
     private double hourlyRate;  
  
     public Money calculatePay() {  
       int straightTime = Math.min(tenthsWorked, TENTHS_PER_WEEK);  
       int overTime = tenthsWorked - straightTime;  
       return new Money(  
         hourlyRate \* (tenthsWorked + OVERTIME_RATE \* overTime)  
       );  
     }  
     …  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_filepos1152783"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **J3: *Constants versus Enums***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Now that <span class="calibre28">`enum`</span>s have been added to the
language (Java 5), use them! Don’t keep using the old trick of
<span class="calibre28">`public static final int`</span>s. The meaning
of <span class="calibre28">`int`</span>s can get lost. The meaning of
<span class="calibre28">`enum`</span>s cannot, because they belong to an
enumeration that is named.

What’s more, study the syntax for <span class="calibre28">`enum`</span>s
carefully. They can have methods and fields. This makes them very
powerful tools that allow much more expression and flexibility than
<span class="calibre28">`int`</span>s. Consider this variation on the
payroll code:

<span class="calibre28"><span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_filepos1153763"
class="calibre2"></span>   public class HourlyEmployee extends Employee
{  
     private int tenthsWorked;  
     **HourlyPayGrade grade;**  
  
     public Money calculatePay() {  
       int straightTime = Math.min(tenthsWorked, TENTHS_PER_WEEK);  
       int overTime = tenthsWorked - straightTime;  
       return new Money(  
         **grade.rate()** \* (tenthsWorked + OVERTIME_RATE \*
overTime)  
       );  
     }  
     …  
   }  
   public enum HourlyPayGrade {  
     APPRENTICE {  
       public double rate() {  
         return 1.0;  
       }  
     },  
     LEUTENANT_JOURNEYMAN {  
       public double rate() {  
         return 1.2;  
       }  
     },  
     JOURNEYMAN {  
       public double rate() {  
         return 1.5;  
       }  
     },  
     MASTER {  
       public double rate() {  
         return 2.0;  
       }  
       };  
  
       public abstract double rate();  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_filepos1155904"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_159.html_calibre_pb_198" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html"></span>

### **Names**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1156053"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **N1: *Choose Descriptive Names***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Don’t be too quick to choose a name. Make sure the name is descriptive.
Remember that meanings tend to drift as software evolves, so frequently
reevaluate the appropriateness of the names you choose.

This is not just a “feel-good” recommendation. Names in software are 90
percent of what make software readable. You need to take the time to
choose them wisely and keep them relevant. Names are too important to
treat carelessly.

Consider the code below. What does it do? If I show you the code with
well-chosen names, it will make perfect sense to you, but like this it’s
just a hodge-podge of symbols and magic numbers.

<span class="calibre28"><span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1157046"
class="calibre2"></span>   public int x() {  
       int q = 0;  
       int z = 0;  
       for (int kk = 0; kk \< 10; kk++) {  
         if (l\[z\] == 10)  
         {  
           q += 10 + (l\[z + 1\] + l\[z + 2\]);  
           z += 1;  
         }  
         else if (l\[z\] + l\[z + 1\] == 10)  
         {  
           q += 10 + l\[z + 2\];  
           z += 2;  
         } else {  
           q += l\[z\] + l\[z + 1\];  
           z += 2;  
         }  
       }  
       return q;  
     }</span>

Here is the code the way it should be written. This snippet is actually
less complete than the one above. Yet you can infer immediately what it
is trying to do, and you could very likely write the missing functions
based on that inferred meaning. The magic numbers are no longer magic,
and the structure of the algorithm is compellingly descriptive.

<span class="calibre28">   public int score() {  
     int score = 0;  
     int frame = 0;  
     for (int frameNumber = 0; frameNumber \< 10; frameNumber++) {  
       if (isStrike(frame)) {  
         score += 10 + nextTwoBallsForStrike(frame);  
         frame += 1;  
       } else if (isSpare(frame)) {  
         score += 10 + nextBallForSpare(frame);  
         frame += 2;  
       } else {  
         score += twoBallsInFrame(frame);  
         frame += 2;  
       }  
     }  
     return score;  
   }</span>

The power of carefully chosen names is that they overload the structure
of the code with description. That overloading sets the readers’
expectations about what the other functions in the module do. You can
infer the implementation of <span class="calibre28">`isStrike()`</span>
by looking at the code above. When you read the
<span class="calibre28">`isStrike`</span> method, it will be “pretty
much what you expected.”<sup>13</sup>

<span class="calibre34">13. See Ward Cunningham’s quote on page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_013.html_filepos120351"
class="calibre18">11</a>.</span>

<span class="calibre28">   private boolean isStrike(int frame) {  
     return rolls\[frame\] == 10;  
   }</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1160938"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1160991" class="calibre2"></span>**N2: *Choose Names at the Appropriate Level of Abstraction***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Don’t pick names that communicate implementation; choose names the
reflect the level of abstraction of the class or function you are
working in. This is hard to do. Again, people are just too good at
mixing levels of abstractions. Each time you make a pass over your code,
you will likely find some variable that is named at too low a level. You
should take the opportunity to change those names when you find them.
Making code readable requires a dedication to continuous improvement.
Consider the <span class="calibre28">`Modem`</span> interface below:

<span class="calibre28">   public interface Modem {  
     boolean dial(String phoneNumber);  
     boolean disconnect();  
     boolean send(char c);  
     char recv();  
     String getConnectedPhoneNumber();  
   }</span>

At first this looks fine. The functions all seem appropriate. Indeed,
for many applications they are. But now consider an application in which
some modems aren’t connected by dialling. Rather they are connected
permanently by hard wiring them together (think of the cable modems that
provide Internet access to most homes nowadays). Perhaps some are
connected by sending a port number to a switch over a USB connection.
Clearly the notion of phone numbers is at the wrong level of
abstraction. A better naming strategy for this scenario might be:

<span class="calibre28">   public interface Modem {  
     boolean connect(String connectionLocator);  
     boolean disconnect();  
     boolean send(char c);  
     char recv();  
     String getConnectedLocator();  
   }</span>

Now the names don’t make any commitments about phone numbers. They can
still be used for phone numbers, or they could be used for any other
kind of connection strategy.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1163482"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **N3: *Use Standard Nomenclature Where Possible***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Names are easier to understand if they are based on existing convention
or usage. For example, if you are using the
D<span class="small">ECORATOR</span> pattern, you should use the word
<span class="calibre28">`Decorator`</span> in the names of the
decorating classes. For example,
<span class="calibre28">`AutoHangupModemDecorator`</span> might be the
name of a class that decorates a <span class="calibre28">`Modem`</span>
with the ability to automatically hang up at the end of a session.

Patterns are just one kind of standard. In Java, for example, functions
that convert objects to string representations are often named
<span class="calibre28">`toString`</span>. It is better to follow
conventions like these than to invent your own.

Teams will often invent their own standard system of names for a
particular project. Eric Evans refers to this as a *ubiquitous language*
for the project.<sup>14</sup> Your code should use
<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1164708"
class="calibre2"></span>the terms from this language extensively. In
short, the more you can use names that are overloaded with special
meanings that are relevant to your project, the easier it will be for
readers to know what your code is talking about.

<span class="calibre34">14.
\[<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1165022"
class="calibre2"></span><a href="#Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1177391"
class="calibre18">DDD</a>\].</span>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1165125"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **N4: *Unambiguous Names***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Choose names that make the workings of a function or variable
unambiguous. Consider this example from FitNesse:

<span class="calibre28">   private String doRename() throws Exception  
   {  
     if(refactorReferences)  
       renameReferences();  
     renamePage();  
  
     pathToRename.removeNameFromEnd();  
     pathToRename.addNameToEnd(newName);  
     return PathParser.render(pathToRename);  
   }</span>

The name of this function does not say what the function does except in
broad and vague terms. This is emphasized by the fact that there is a
function named <span class="calibre28">`renamePage`</span> inside the
function named <span class="calibre28">`doRename`</span>! What do the
names tell you about the difference between the two functions? Nothing.

A better name for that function is
<span class="calibre28">`renamePageAndOptionallyAllReferences`</span>.
This may seem long, and it is, but it’s only called from one place in
the module, so it’s explanatory value outweighs the length.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1166747"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **N5: *Use Long Names for Long Scopes***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

The length of a name should be related to the length of the scope. You
can use very short variable names for tiny scopes, but for big scopes
you should use longer names.

Variable names like <span class="calibre28">`i`</span> and
<span class="calibre28">`j`</span> are just fine if their scope is five
lines long. Consider this snippet from the old standard “Bowling Game”:

<span class="calibre28">   private void rollMany(int n, int pins)  
   {  
     for (int i=0; i\<n; i++)  
       g.roll(pins);  
   }</span>

This is perfectly clear and would be obfuscated if the variable
<span class="calibre28">`i`</span> were replaced with something annoying
like <span class="calibre28">`rollCount`</span>. On the other hand,
variables and functions with short names lose their meaning over long
distances. So the longer the scope of the name, the longer and more
precise the name should be.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1168154"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **N6: *Avoid Encodings***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Names should not be encoded with type or scope information. Prefixes
such as <span class="calibre28">`m_`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`f`</span> are useless in today’s environments.
Also project and/or subsystem encodings such as
<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1168556"
class="calibre2"></span><span class="calibre28">`vis_`</span> (for
visual imaging system) are distracting and redundant. Again, today’s
environments provide all that information without having to mangle the
names. Keep your names free of Hungarian pollution.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1168862"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **N7: *Names Should Describe Side-Effects***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Names should describe everything that a function, variable, or class is
or does. Don’t hide side effects with a name. Don’t use a simple verb to
describe a function that does more than just that simple action. For
example, consider this code from TestNG:

<span class="calibre28">   public ObjectOutputStream getOos() throws
IOException {  
     if (m_oos == null) {  
       m_oos = new ObjectOutputStream(m_socket.getOutputStream());  
     }  
     return m_oos;  
   }</span>

This function does a bit more than get an “oos”; it creates the “oos” if
it hasn’t been created already. Thus, a better name might be
<span class="calibre28">`createOrReturnOos`</span>.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_filepos1170055"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_160.html_calibre_pb_199" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html"></span>

### **Tests**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1170204"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T1: *Insufficient Tests***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

How many tests should be in a test suite? Unfortunately, the metric many
programmers use is “That seems like enough.” A test suite should test
everything that could possibly break. The tests are insufficient so long
as there are conditions that have not been explored by the tests or
calculations that have not been validated.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1170758"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T2: *Use a Coverage Tool!***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Coverage tools reports gaps in your testing strategy. They make it easy
to find modules, classes, and functions that are insufficiently tested.
Most IDEs give you a visual indication, marking lines that are covered
in green and those that are uncovered in red. This makes it quick and
easy to find <span class="calibre28">`if`</span> or
<span class="calibre28">`catch`</span> statements whose bodies haven’t
been checked.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1171407"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T3: *Don’t Skip Trivial Tests***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

They are easy to write and their documentary value is higher than the
cost to produce them.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1171726"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T4: *An Ignored Test Is a Question about an Ambiguity***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Sometimes we are uncertain about a behavioral detail because the
requirements are unclear. We can express our question about the
requirements as a test that is commented out, or as a test that
annotated with <span class="calibre28">`@Ignore`</span>. Which you
choose depends upon whether the ambiguity is about something that would
compile or not.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1172321"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### <span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1172374" class="calibre2"></span>**T5: *Test Boundary Conditions***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Take special care to test boundary conditions. We often get the middle
of an algorithm right but misjudge the boundaries.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1172671"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T6: *Exhaustively Test Near Bugs***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Bugs tend to congregate. When you find a bug in a function, it is wise
to do an exhaustive test of that function. You’ll probably find that the
bug was not alone.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1173064"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T7: *Patterns of Failure Are Revealing***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Sometimes you can diagnose a problem by finding patterns in the way the
test cases fail. This is another argument for making the test cases as
complete as possible. Complete test cases, ordered in a reasonable way,
expose patterns.

As a simple example, suppose you noticed that all tests with an input
larger than five characters failed? Or what if any test that passed a
negative number into the second argument of a function failed? Sometimes
just seeing the pattern of red and green on the test report is enough to
spark the “Aha!” that leads to the solution. Look back at page
<a href="#Clean_Code_split_149.html_filepos992872"
class="calibre18">267</a> to see an interesting example of this in the
<span class="calibre28">`SerialDate`</span> example.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1174074"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T8: *Test Coverage Patterns Can Be Revealing***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

Looking at the code that is or is not executed by the passing tests
gives clues to why the failing tests fail.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1174421"
class="calibre2"></span>

#### **T9: *Tests Should Be Fast***

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

A slow test is a test that won’t get run. When things get tight, it’s
the slow tests that will be dropped from the suite. So *do what you
must* to keep your tests fast.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_filepos1174826"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_161.html_calibre_pb_200" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_162.html"></span>

### **Conclusion**

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

This list of heuristics and smells could hardly be said to be complete.
Indeed, I’m not sure that such a list can *ever* be complete. But
perhaps completeness should not be the goal, because what this list
*does* do is imply a value system.

Indeed, that value system has been the goal, and the topic, of this
book. Clean code is not written by following a set of rules. You don’t
become a software craftsman by learning a list of heuristics.
Professionalism and craftsmanship come from values that drive
disciplines.

<span id="Clean_Code_split_162.html_filepos1175635"
class="calibre2"></span>

<div id="Clean_Code_split_162.html_calibre_pb_201" class="mbppagebreak">

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_163.html"></span>

<div class="calibre7">

 

</div>

<span id="Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1177107"

<span id="Clean_Code_split_163.html_filepos1177391"
